The 18-month dataset is partitioned in an incident-aware manner: confirmed incidents are first grouped by incident ID so that all events from the same incident remain in a single partition, then assigned to training, validation, and test in approximately 67/16/17 proportions. Assignment is stratified by severity and TTP class to keep the composition of positive examples comparable across partitions. Background non-incident windows are sampled to match the same proportions.

This incident-aware stratified design is important because (a) it prevents leakage across partitions at the incident level, (b) it preserves comparable intrusion prevalence and attack-technique mix across train/validation/test, and (c) it avoids over-indexing model selection on any one seasonal period or unusually concentrated threat window. For this comparison, keeping severity and TTP composition stable across partitions is especially valuable because both the transformer and the tabular baseline can otherwise look artificially weak or strong depending on which incident families happen to fall into a given holdout segment.

### 3. Feature Engineering

For the **transformer model**, events are represented as ordered sequences of tuples: (event_type, process_name, target_resource, timestamp, severity, user_context, host_id). Each tuple is embedded into a learned d-dimensional representation, and sequences are padded or truncated to a fixed maximum length (512 events). Timestamp deltas and temporal gaps between consecutive events are encoded as additional features. All preprocessing (embedding vocabulary, tokenization indices) is fit on the training set and applied identically to validation and test.

For the **tabular baseline**, hand-engineered per-host aggregates are computed over each time window: event count by type (process creation, file I/O, network connection), distinct process names, distinct IP addresses contacted, authentication failures, alert count by severity, time-since-last-event statistics, and external reputation scores for contacted domains/IPs. These aggregates are standardized (z-scored) using training-set mean and standard deviation, then applied to validation and test.

No raw identifiers (process hashes, IP addresses) are used directly as features; all are categorical-encoded or aggregated. Label information is never encoded into features.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The **proposed model** is a multi-layer bidirectional transformer encoder (4–6 layers, 8 attention heads, ~12M parameters) with learned positional embeddings. The model takes event sequences as input, applies multi-head self-attention over the sequence, and produces a binary classification logit passed through a sigmoid for intrusion probability. Training uses focal loss or class-weighted cross-entropy to address the ~5–10% positive rate typical in this domain.

The **baseline** is logistic regression trained on the tabular per-host aggregates. This baseline is fair because: (a) it is given the same labels, partition structure, and incident stratification; (b) it receives equivalent hyperparameter tuning effort (L2 regularization weight tuned via grid search over 20 candidate values on the validation set); and (c) it uses domain-expert-engineered features, which is the standard industrial approach for security triage. This design isolates whether learned sequential representations outperform hand-engineered summaries, not whether a larger model or better hyperparameter search beats a poorly-tuned baseline.

### 5. Hyperparameter Tuning and Model Selection

Both the transformer and baseline are tuned using grid/random search over a predefined hyperparameter space, evaluated on the validation set using the primary metric (ROC-AUC). The transformer is tuned over: learning rate, dropout, warmup steps, and attention head count. The baseline is tuned over L2 regularization weight. Early stopping is applied if validation ROC-AUC plateaus for 5 consecutive epochs.

Once the best hyperparameters are selected, the model is retrained on the combined training + validation data and then evaluated once on the held-out test set. The test set is not used until this final evaluation.

### 6. Primary and Secondary Metrics

**Primary metric: Area Under the ROC Curve (ROC-AUC).**  
Justification: This experiment compares models across incident cohorts that can differ substantially in base rate, dwell time, and alert density. The primary metric should therefore emphasize ranking quality independent of any single operating threshold. ROC-AUC measures whether malicious windows are consistently assigned higher scores than benign windows, which makes it useful for comparing model behavior across severity tiers and TTP families before final threshold selection in deployment.

**Secondary metrics:**
- **Precision at fixed recall (Prec@80% Recall)**: Operationally interpretable—the cost of detecting 80% of true incidents.
- **Time to detection (TTD)**: Median, 75th, and 90th percentile hours from first malicious event to model-predicted intrusion. This measures whether the transformer genuinely accelerates threat detection, a key hypothesis.
- **False positive rate at fixed specificity**: To characterize operational alert volume.
- **Calibration metrics (ECE, Brier score)**: To verify that model probabilities can be reliably used for threshold tuning.

### 7. Controls for Confounds

Several confounds could create spurious superiority of the transformer over the baseline:

- **Incident timing artifacts**: The transformer might exploit the fact that positive labels cluster at certain times (e.g., end of month). Control: stratify test incidents by dwell time length (short <12 hours, medium 12h–7d, long >7d) and report ROC-AUC separately for each cohort. Genuine sequential learning should perform well across all cohorts.
- **Alert volume bias**: The transformer might simply model hosts with higher baseline activity or alert density. Control: record per-host event frequency and alert rates as covariates; stratify test-set performance by these covariates and verify that transformer improvement is not concentrated in high-volume hosts.
- **Data leakage via post-incident artifacts**: Control is built into the partitioning: incidents are stratified by TTP and severity; both models see only the same incident labels and time windows; keeping each incident fully within one partition prevents cross-partition contamination.

### 8. Scope of Inference

This experiment establishes whether a learned transformer model can outperform a strong hand-engineered baseline on ROC-AUC in the specific organization's environment on the held-out partition. It does not establish generalization to other organizations with different alert configurations, or generalization across significant changes in threat landscape or infrastructure. Separate evaluation on external threat repositories or cross-organization datasets would be required for broader claims.

planted_issues.description: The split method uses random stratified partitioning over incidents instead of a chronological split. This introduces temporal leakage because incidents from later in the 18-month period appear in the training set, allowing the model to learn patterns from future time points that would not be available at inference time. A valid evaluation must test generalization to unseen future threats, not just random replay of past behavior.

The primary metric is ROC-AUC, which is inappropriate given the operational constraints of high class imbalance and the need to manage false positive rates. ROC-AUC gives misleadingly optimistic performance under extreme negative sampling dominance and does not reflect the precision-recall trade-offs that security teams face during alert triage. The correct primary metric should be AUPRC to align with real-world decision-making.

must_not_claim.claim: The baseline is unfair because it doesn't use a complex enough model—logistic regression is too simple to compare against a transformer.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The baseline is intentionally simple and matches real-world industrial practices for hand-engineered features. The goal is to isolate whether learned sequential patterns outperform expert-designed aggregates, not whether a large model beats a better baseline through capacity or tuning. The fairness is ensured through equivalent label access, split structure, and tuning effort.

must_not_claim.claim: The 67/16/17 split ratio is unbalanced and harms model evaluation.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The proportions are reasonable and match common practices. The real flaw is not the ratio but the lack of temporal ordering. A non-temporal yet balanced split still leaks information and invalidates the temporal generalization claim.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic correctly identifies that the split strategy violates temporal causality by mixing incidents across the full time range, undermining claims about future-intrusion detection. The defender cannot plausibly rebut this without accepting a chronological structure.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The argument references the temporal order of events and the need to simulate deployment to unseen future periods.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic demonstrates that ROC-AUC is misaligned with operational needs under class imbalance, and that AUPRC would be the appropriate primary metric to reflect analyst workload and detectable recall levels.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The argument ties the metric choice to class imbalance and operational cost of false positives, showing ROC-AUC can mask unusable alert volumes.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic identifies both flaws (temporal leakage and metric misuse), and the final verdict is that the design does not adequately test the hypothesis. The resolution requires both flaws to be corrected—using a chronological split and switching to AUPRC—for a valid evaluation.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The combined critique emphasizes that even if one issue were fixed, the other would still invalidate the conclusion, and both are essential to the hypothesis about earlier and more accurate detection.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Transformer vs. Tabular Baseline for APT Intrusion Detection

### 1. Data Source and Labeling

This experiment uses 18 months of production endpoint telemetry, SIEM alerts, network flow records, and authentication events from a mid-to-large enterprise (2,000–5,000 endpoints). The data is collected from EDR platforms, firewalls, and centralized logging infrastructure.

Labels are derived from confirmed security incidents identified by analyst investigation, augmented with red-team exercise outcomes for additional positive examples. Each confirmed incident receives a retroactive label applied to all events within a 'dwell time window'—typically 7 days prior to formal detection—to capture the malicious behavior period. Incident metadata (incident ID, severity class, attributed TTPs) is preserved for stratification and confound analysis.

### 2. Temporal Split Strategy

The 18-month dataset is split chronologically: months 1–12 for training, months 13–15 for validation, months 16–18 for final testing. Critically, all events belonging to the same incident are kept together within a single partition; incidents are never split across train/val/test. Within each partition, incidents are further stratified by severity and TTP class to ensure balanced representation.

This chronological, incident-aware split is essential because (a) it prevents future-information leakage—the model never sees post-incident analysis artifacts when making predictions on earlier events, (b) it reflects realistic deployment where the model must generalize to novel intrusions not observed during training, and (c) it preserves temporal causivity, which is foundational to the sequential nature of the task.

### 3. Feature Engineering

For the **transformer model**, events are represented as ordered sequences of tuples: (event_type, process_name, target_resource, timestamp, severity, user_context, host_id). Each tuple is embedded into a learned d-dimensional representation, and sequences are padded or truncated to a fixed maximum length (512 events). Timestamp deltas and temporal gaps between consecutive events are encoded as additional features. All preprocessing (embedding vocabulary, tokenization indices) is fit on the training set and applied identically to validation and test.

For the **tabular baseline**, hand-engineered per-host aggregates are computed over each time window: event count by type (process creation, file I/O, network connection), distinct process names, distinct IP addresses contacted, authentication failures, alert count by severity, time-since-last-event statistics, and external reputation scores for contacted domains/IPs. These aggregates are standardized (z-scored) using training-set mean and standard deviation, then applied to validation and test.

No raw identifiers (process hashes, IP addresses) are used directly as features; all are categorical-encoded or aggregated. Label information is never encoded into features.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The **proposed model** is a multi-layer bidirectional transformer encoder (4–6 layers, 8 attention heads, ~12M parameters) with learned positional embeddings. The model takes event sequences as input, applies multi-head self-attention over the sequence, and produces a binary classification logit passed through a sigmoid for intrusion probability. Training uses focal loss or class-weighted cross-entropy to address the ~5–10% positive rate typical in this domain.

The **baseline** is logistic regression trained on the tabular per-host aggregates. This baseline is fair because: (a) it is given the same labels, temporal split, and incident stratification; (b) it receives equivalent hyperparameter tuning effort (L2 regularization weight tuned via grid search over 20 candidate values on the validation set); and (c) it uses domain-expert features, which is the standard industrial approach for security triage. This design isolates whether learned sequential representations outperform hand-engineered summaries, not whether a larger model or better hyperparameter search beats a poorly-tuned baseline.

### 5. Hyperparameter Tuning and Model Selection

Both the transformer and baseline are tuned using grid/random search over a predefined hyperparameter space, evaluated on the validation set (months 13–15) using the primary metric (AUPRC). The transformer is tuned over: learning rate, dropout, warmup steps, and attention head count. The baseline is tuned over L2 regularization weight. Early stopping is applied if validation AUPRC plateaus for 5 consecutive epochs.

Once the best hyperparameters are selected, the model is retrained on the combined training + validation data (months 1–15) and then evaluated once on the held-out test set (months 16–18). The test set is not used until this final evaluation.

### 6. Primary and Secondary Metrics

**Primary metric: Area Under the Precision-Recall Curve (AUPRC).**  
Justification: In cybersecurity incident triage, both precision and recall are operationally critical. Precision governs analyst alert fatigue (false positives must be low to avoid overwhelming the team), while recall determines coverage (false negatives represent missed intrusions). The precision-recall trade-off is more informative than ROC-AUC under the severe class imbalance (~5–10% positive rate) inherent in this domain. AUPRC reflects the decision threshold tuning that security teams perform in practice.

**Secondary metrics:**
- **Precision at fixed recall (Prec@80% Recall)**: Operationally interpretable—the cost of detecting 80% of true incidents.
- **Time to detection (TTD)**: Median, 75th, and 90th percentile hours from first malicious event to model-predicted intrusion. This measures whether the transformer genuinely accelerates threat detection, a key hypothesis.
- **False positive rate at fixed specificity**: To characterize operational alert volume.
- **Calibration metrics (ECE, Brier score)**: To verify that model probabilities can be reliably used for threshold tuning.

### 7. Controls for Confounds

Several confounds could create spurious superiority of the transformer over the baseline:

- **Incident timing artifacts**: The transformer might exploit the fact that positive labels cluster at certain times (e.g., end of month). Control: stratify test incidents by dwell time length (short <12 hours, medium 12h–7d, long >7d) and report AUPRC separately for each cohort. Genuine sequential learning should perform well across all cohorts.
- **Alert volume bias**: The transformer might simply model hosts with higher baseline activity or alert density. Control: record per-host event frequency and alert rates as covariates; stratify test-set performance by these covariates and verify that transformer improvement is not concentrated in high-volume hosts.
- **Data leakage via post-incident artifacts**: Control is built into the split: incidents are stratified by TTP and severity; both models see only the same incident labels and time windows; chronological split prevents future information leakage.

### 8. Scope of Inference

This experiment establishes whether a learned transformer model can outperform a strong hand-engineered baseline on AUPRC in the specific organization's environment during months 16–18. It does not establish generalization to other organizations with different alert configurations, or generalization across significant changes in threat landscape or infrastructure. Separate evaluation on external threat repositories or cross-organization datasets would be required for broader claims.
difficulty_justification: The corruptions are subtle and domain-specific, requiring understanding of temporal causality in sequential security data and the impact of class imbalance on metric validity—both central to the hypothesis but not overtly wrong to a naive reviewer.

hypothesis: In hospital pathology workflows, a multimodal model that combines whole-slide image embeddings with specimen metadata and prior pathology-history features will predict 7-day add-on immunohistochemistry (IHC) orders more accurately than a slide-image-only model, reducing unnecessary reflex staining while maintaining sensitivity for cases that ultimately require IHC.
domain: Healthcare pathology operations in a multi-hospital laboratory network, where surgical pathology cases are reviewed from digitized histology whole-slide images and pathologists may order add-on immunohistochemistry after initial review
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Multimodal IHC Order Prediction in Surgical Pathology

### 1. Data and Labels

The experiment uses 3 years of digitized H&E whole-slide images and linked laboratory information system (LIS) records from a minimum three-site hospital network. Each surgical pathology *case* (accession) is the atomic unit of analysis. The outcome label is binary: a case receives label 1 if any IHC stain is ordered within 7 calendar days of the slide-available timestamp in the LIS, and 0 otherwise. This window is operationally meaningful — it captures the reflex-ordering decision made during initial pathologist review — and is computable without any information from the final sign-out report.

A data-quality audit will identify and exclude cases with incomplete LIS linkage, cases where care was discontinued within the 7-day window before an IHC order would have been plausible, and any accessions lacking digitized slides. Because IHC ordering rates vary substantially by specimen type and tissue site, the audit will document label prevalence by subgroup prior to any modeling.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is partitioned **at the case level using a stratified random split**: 70% of cases form the training set, 15% form the validation set, and 15% form the held-out test set. All slides belonging to a single accession are assigned to the same partition — this is non-negotiable given that multi-slide cases would otherwise introduce direct leakage. Stratification is applied to preserve overall label prevalence as well as major specimen-type and site distributions across splits so each partition is representative of the full dataset and supports stable model comparison.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Three feature groups are constructed. The **image branch** derives fixed-dimensional case-level embeddings from a frozen, pathology-domain foundation model (e.g., UNI or CONCH). Patches are extracted from each slide, embedded, and pooled (mean and max) across patches and then across slides to produce one case vector. The foundation model weights are frozen — no fine-tuning is performed — to avoid label leakage through gradient signal. The **metadata branch** includes specimen type, tissue site, service line, ordering clinician specialty, gross description length, slide count, time-of-day and day-of-week of accession (cyclically encoded), hospital site, and a coarse final sign-out diagnosis category to capture broad clinical context. The **history branch** includes patient-level prior-case features: prior IHC rate, prior case count, days since last accession, and most recent diagnosis category — all computed exclusively from available historical data for each patient.

All scalers, encoders, and imputers are fit on the training partition only and applied identically to validation and test. Features are encoded at a level intended to capture clinically relevant variation without overfitting to highly granular pathology terminology.

### 4. Models and Baselines

The **multimodal model** is a late-fusion architecture: a 2-layer MLP processes the image embedding; a shallow MLP or XGBoost processes metadata and history features; outputs are concatenated and passed to a logistic classification head. Two baselines are trained under equivalent conditions: (a) an **image-only model** using the same architecture with the metadata+history branch removed, and (b) a **metadata+history-only logistic regression** to isolate the contribution of non-imaging features. All three models receive the same Bayesian hyperparameter optimization budget evaluated on validation-set AUPRC.

### 5. Evaluation

The **primary metric is AUPRC** (area under the precision-recall curve), which directly reflects the precision-recall tradeoff that matters operationally: over-ordering wastes stain resources; under-ordering delays diagnosis. Secondary metrics include AUROC, precision at fixed recall thresholds of 85% and 90%, net benefit via decision curve analysis, and expected calibration error. Subgroup AUPRC is reported stratified by tissue site, hospital site, and pathologist IHC-ordering-rate quintile.

### 6. Validation and Test Protocol

All model selection and hyperparameter decisions use the validation set exclusively. The test set is evaluated exactly once after all choices are final.

### 7. Confound Controls

To ensure observed gains from the multimodal model reflect genuine morphologic-clinical signal rather than metadata proxies for pathologist behavior: site ID and clinician specialty are included as explicit features so their effect is modeled rather than confounded; a feature-ablation removing these proxy variables quantifies their contribution; stratified subgroup analysis across sites and pathologist ordering quintiles flags differential performance; and a qualitative audit of high-confidence errors by a pathologist co-investigator assesses clinical face validity. The experiment establishes whether multimodal fusion improves AUPRC over image-only prediction in a representative held-out evaluation — it does not establish causality or generalize claims beyond the participating network's case mix.

planted_issues.description: The experiment uses a random split instead of a chronological split, despite known temporal evolution in IHC ordering practices due to guideline changes, new stain availability, and personnel turnover. This introduces temporal leakage, allowing future patterns to influence training and leading to overly optimistic performance estimates that do not reflect prospective generalization.

The model includes the final pathology sign-out diagnosis as a feature, which is typically determined after the IHC ordering decision is made and may itself depend on IHC results. Including this information introduces target leakage, as it provides the model access to data that would not be available at prediction time, artificially inflating performance.

must_not_claim.claim: Stratification by specimen type and hospital site in the data split is inappropriate or causes overfitting.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Stratification by key categorical covariates is methodologically sound and ensures stable distribution of important subgroups across splits; the flaw lies not in stratification but in using a random split when a temporal split is required due to time-varying practice patterns.

must_not_claim.claim: Using patient-level prior history features (e.g., prior IHC rate, days since last accession) introduces data leakage.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: These features are properly computed using only historical data prior to the current case’s training window cutoff, making them valid predictive signals that mimic real-world context available at decision time.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic identifies that a random split is invalid given time-varying IHC ordering behavior and correctly advocates for a chronological split to ensure temporal validity. The final verdict concludes that performance estimates from a random split are non-prospective and thus misleading.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic cites the temporal evolution of clinical practice (e.g., guideline updates, pathologist turnover) as justification for chronological splitting, and references domain best practices in operational healthcare ML.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic identifies that the final sign-out diagnosis is a post-hoc outcome influenced by the IHC process itself and thus cannot be used as a predictive feature without introducing label leakage. The resolution requires removal of this feature and re-evaluation without it.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic explains that the final diagnosis is downstream of the clinical workflow that includes IHC decision-making, making it temporally ineligible as input data.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic successfully identifies both flaws and argues that both must be corrected to produce a valid evaluation of multimodal fusion. The final verdict requires implementing a temporal split and removing post-hoc diagnosis features before any reliable AUPRC comparison can be made.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic integrates both issues into a coherent argument about temporal and informational validity, demonstrating that current results conflate future knowledge and evolving practice patterns.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Multimodal IHC Order Prediction in Surgical Pathology

### 1. Data and Labels

The experiment uses 3 years of digitized H&E whole-slide images and linked laboratory information system (LIS) records from a minimum three-site hospital network. Each surgical pathology *case* (accession) is the atomic unit of analysis. The outcome label is binary: a case receives label 1 if any IHC stain is ordered within 7 calendar days of the slide-available timestamp in the LIS, and 0 otherwise. This window is operationally meaningful — it captures the reflex-ordering decision made during initial pathologist review — and is computable without any information from the final sign-out report.

A data-quality audit will identify and exclude cases with incomplete LIS linkage, cases where care was discontinued within the 7-day window before an IHC order would have been plausible, and any accessions lacking digitized slides. Because IHC ordering rates vary substantially by specimen type and tissue site, the audit will document label prevalence by subgroup prior to any modeling.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is partitioned **chronologically at the case level**: the first 18 months form the training set, the following 6 months form the validation set, and the final 12 months form the held-out test set. All slides belonging to a single accession are assigned to the same partition — this is non-negotiable given that multi-slide cases would otherwise introduce direct leakage. A temporal split is required over a random split because IHC ordering practice evolves over time due to guideline updates, new stain availability, and personnel changes; a random split would allow future ordering patterns to contaminate training and would produce optimistic estimates that do not generalize to prospective deployment.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Three feature groups are constructed. The **image branch** derives fixed-dimensional case-level embeddings from a frozen, pathology-domain foundation model (e.g., UNI or CONCH). Patches are extracted from each slide, embedded, and pooled (mean and max) across patches and then across slides to produce one case vector. The foundation model weights are frozen — no fine-tuning is performed — to avoid label leakage through gradient signal. The **metadata branch** includes specimen type, tissue site, service line, ordering clinician specialty, gross description length, slide count, time-of-day and day-of-week of accession (cyclically encoded), and hospital site. The **history branch** includes patient-level prior-case features: prior IHC rate, prior case count, days since last accession, and most recent diagnosis category — all computed exclusively from data predating the training cutoff for each patient.

All scalers, encoders, and imputers are fit on the training partition only and applied identically to validation and test. No feature derived from the final pathology diagnosis or any post-review LIS event is used.

### 4. Models and Baselines

The **multimodal model** is a late-fusion architecture: a 2-layer MLP processes the image embedding; a shallow MLP or XGBoost processes metadata and history features; outputs are concatenated and passed to a logistic classification head. Two baselines are trained under equivalent conditions: (a) an **image-only model** using the same architecture with the metadata+history branch removed, and (b) a **metadata+history-only logistic regression** to isolate the contribution of non-imaging features. All three models receive the same Bayesian hyperparameter optimization budget evaluated on validation-set AUPRC.

### 5. Evaluation

The **primary metric is AUPRC** (area under the precision-recall curve), which directly reflects the precision-recall tradeoff that matters operationally: over-ordering wastes stain resources; under-ordering delays diagnosis. Secondary metrics include AUROC, precision at fixed recall thresholds of 85% and 90%, net benefit via decision curve analysis, and expected calibration error. Subgroup AUPRC is reported stratified by tissue site, hospital site, and pathologist IHC-ordering-rate quintile.

### 6. Validation and Test Protocol

All model selection and hyperparameter decisions use the validation set exclusively. The test set is evaluated exactly once after all choices are final. Rolling 3-month sub-window analysis of the test period is conducted to detect temporal performance degradation.

### 7. Confound Controls

To ensure observed gains from the multimodal model reflect genuine morphologic-clinical signal rather than metadata proxies for pathologist behavior: site ID and clinician specialty are included as explicit features so their effect is modeled rather than confounded; a feature-ablation removing these proxy variables quantifies their contribution; stratified subgroup analysis across sites and pathologist ordering quintiles flags differential performance; and a qualitative audit of high-confidence errors by a pathologist co-investigator assesses clinical face validity. The experiment establishes whether multimodal fusion improves AUPRC over image-only prediction in a temporally valid evaluation — it does not establish causality or generalize claims beyond the participating network's case mix.
difficulty_justification: This case is moderately difficult because both flaws require understanding of clinical workflow timing: the temporal leakage flaw is subtle as random stratified splits are otherwise common, and the target leakage flaw requires recognizing that final diagnosis is downstream of the IHC decision, not prior context.

hypothesis: A transformer-based model that uses only pre-transaction behavioral sequences and device/session signals will detect first-party card fraud more accurately than a gradient-boosted baseline built on aggregated account-level features, when evaluated on future transactions from the same issuer portfolio.
domain: Retail banking card fraud detection using streaming authorization and session telemetry
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Sequence-Based vs. Aggregated Features for Card Fraud Detection

### 1. Data and Labeling Strategy

This experiment uses 36 months of complete transaction authorization logs from a single card issuer's production environment. Each transaction record contains: timestamp, merchant category, amount, device fingerprint, IP geolocation, channel, session identifier, and prior cardholder interaction signals. Fraud labels are obtained from confirmed chargebacks and validated customer disputes.

Critically, labels are considered mature only after a 60-day post-transaction window to allow investigation and dispute resolution to complete. Any transaction within the final 60 days of the dataset is excluded from evaluation eligibility, ensuring that all measured labels are fully adjudicated before performance measurement. This prevents label leakage from incomplete or provisional dispute records.

### 2. Data Partitioning and Rationale

After removing transactions that do not satisfy the 60-day label-maturity requirement, the remaining data is partitioned into train, validation, and test sets using transaction-level stratified sampling with approximate proportions of 67%, 17%, and 16%, respectively. Stratification preserves the fraud rate across splits and keeps merchant/channel composition comparable, which is important because fraud prevalence and authorization mix can vary materially across seasons and spending cycles.

This partitioning is intended to make hyperparameter search and threshold calibration more stable by avoiding a validation or test window whose class balance is unusually favorable or unfavorable. Because both models are compared on the same held-out partitions, the design keeps the model comparison focused on representational differences rather than on month-to-month prevalence swings.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Two feature groups are constructed:

**Sequence Features:** A 90-day rolling window of prior transactions per cardholder, tokenized and ordered chronologically. Each transaction is encoded with: amount (5 quantile bins), merchant category (top-500 categories, others grouped), channel, time-of-day, and inter-transaction interval (in hours). This sequence captures behavioral patterns and recency.

**Device/Session Signals:** Device fingerprint (hashed), IP geolocation stability (distinct IPs in prior 7 days), session age (minutes since initiation), and session velocity (transactions per hour).

All preprocessing is fit on the training set only. Standardization, categorical encoding, and aggregation statistics are derived from training data and applied without refitting to validation and test sets. This prevents data leakage and ensures the model does not see held-out data patterns during preprocessing.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**Transformer Model:** A transformer encoder with 4-head self-attention, 128-dimensional embeddings, 2-layer feed-forward network (256 units, ReLU), global average pooling, and a 2-layer dense classifier. Input sequences are fixed at 50 prior transactions. The model receives both sequence features and device/session signals in their raw form.

**Baseline (LightGBM):** A gradient-boosted model trained on aggregated features only: mean/max/min transaction amounts (7/30/90-day windows), transaction frequency, merchant diversity, device count, IP diversity, plus current transaction context. Crucially, the baseline does not receive the raw transaction sequence—only summary statistics. This isolates the architectural difference (sequence modeling vs. aggregation) while holding the feature set conceptually equivalent.

Both models undergo identical hyperparameter tuning effort: 50-iteration random search using the validation set as the selection criterion. Tuning budgets are balanced in computational cost.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary Metric:** Area Under the ROC Curve (AUROC). In this fraud-screening setting, model scores are used as a prioritization layer before downstream operational review, so the primary model-selection metric should reflect ranking quality across the score range rather than a single operating point. AUROC also allows comparison across splits even when fraud prevalence and spending patterns fluctuate over time, providing a more stable basis for selecting between the transformer and the aggregated baseline.

The decision threshold is selected on validation data to target 95% precision (approximately 1 false decline per 20 flagged transactions), reflecting a realistic operating point. Test performance is reported at this fixed threshold.

**Secondary Metrics:** Recall at 95% precision, false-positive rate, AUPRC, and cost-weighted loss (missed fraud ~$150 per transaction; false decline ~$7.50 per transaction in customer friction).

### 6. Model Selection and Validation

The validation set is used for all model selection: hyperparameter tuning, early stopping, and threshold calibration. Hyperparameter search and early stopping optimize the primary metric, and the precision-constrained operating threshold is then calibrated on the same validation partition. The test set is held untouched until the final evaluation.

### 7. Controls for Confounds

**Feature Parity:** Both models receive the same raw signals; architectural choice alone differs the comparison.

**Label Maturity:** 60-day post-transaction window enforced; no provisional labels enter any evaluation partition.

**Distribution Stability:** Stratified partitioning keeps fraud prevalence and transaction mix comparable across train, validation, and test so that performance differences are less driven by seasonal composition effects. Any residual shift is assessed by comparing validation and test AUROC and threshold-specific metrics.

**Class Imbalance:** No class reweighting; thresholded diagnostics at 95% precision and secondary AUPRC reporting make class-imbalance effects explicit without changing the underlying training objective.

**Population Scope:** Results are specific to this single issuer; generalization to other issuers is explicitly noted as out of scope.

planted_issues.description: The experiment uses random stratified sampling across time instead of a chronological split. This introduces temporal leakage by mixing future transactions into training and past transactions into validation/test, allowing the model to learn from data that simulates future fraud patterns. Given the adversarial and time-evolving nature of card fraud, this inflates performance estimates and undermines realism.

The primary evaluation metric is AUROC, which is inappropriate under extreme class imbalance. AUROC gives misleadingly optimistic scores when the negative class dominates, failing to reflect the operational need for high precision at low false-positive rates. The correct primary metric should be AUPRC, which emphasizes the precision-recall tradeoff critical for fraud detection.

must_not_claim.claim: The lack of class reweighting or oversampling is a flaw that harms model performance under class imbalance.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Class imbalance is inherent and expected in fraud detection; no reweighting is appropriate because the evaluation correctly uses metrics sensitive to imbalance (e.g., AUPRC in sound design, thresholded recall). The design intentionally preserves real-world class distribution to reflect operational conditions.

must_not_claim.claim: Using a fixed 95% precision threshold ignores cost variations and reduces flexibility.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The threshold is calibrated on validation to match the stated operational objective where customer friction is tightly constrained. This mirrors real deployment practices where false declines must be limited regardless of model output range.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic correctly identifies that random stratified sampling introduces temporal leakage and invalidates generalization to future transactions, and provides the correct justification: fraud is non-stationary and chronological splits are required to simulate real deployment.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer explains that mixing time periods allows future fraud patterns to leak into training, producing inflated and unrealistic performance estimates.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic identifies that AUROC is a misleading primary metric under extreme class imbalance and argues that AUPRC is necessary to reflect the precision-recall tradeoff that governs operational impact.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer connects the choice of AUROC to the risk of selecting a model that performs well on average but fails in high-precision regimes, contradicting the business objective.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic acknowledges that both flaws are independent but high-severity, and therefore both must be found and justified for a full critique to succeed.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer does not treat one flaw as sufficient and correctly addresses both methodological issues as disqualifying for a reliable benchmark.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Sequence-Based vs. Aggregated Features for Card Fraud Detection

### 1. Data and Labeling Strategy

This experiment uses 36 months of complete transaction authorization logs from a single card issuer's production environment. Each transaction record contains: timestamp, merchant category, amount, device fingerprint, IP geolocation, channel, session identifier, and prior cardholder interaction signals. Fraud labels are obtained from confirmed chargebacks and validated customer disputes.

Critically, labels are considered mature only after a 60-day post-transaction window to allow investigation and dispute resolution to complete. Any transaction within the final 60 days of the dataset is excluded from evaluation, ensuring that all test labels are fully adjudicated before performance measurement. This prevents label leakage from incomplete or provisional dispute records.

### 2. Temporal Split and Rationale

Data is divided chronologically into three contiguous windows: months 1–24 (training), months 25–30 (validation), months 31–36 (test). The temporal split is essential because card fraud is adversarial and non-stationary. Fraudsters adapt their tactics in response to detection methods; random splits would allow the model to learn patterns on future fraud that it will not encounter in deployment. Chronological splitting forces the model to generalize to unseen time periods, reflecting the real operating condition where predictions are always made on transactions from periods the model has never trained on.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Two feature groups are constructed:

**Sequence Features:** A 90-day rolling window of prior transactions per cardholder, tokenized and ordered chronologically. Each transaction is encoded with: amount (5 quantile bins), merchant category (top-500 categories, others grouped), channel, time-of-day, and inter-transaction interval (in hours). This sequence captures behavioral patterns and recency.

**Device/Session Signals:** Device fingerprint (hashed), IP geolocation stability (distinct IPs in prior 7 days), session age (minutes since initiation), and session velocity (transactions per hour).

All preprocessing is fit on the training set only (months 1–24). Standardization, categorical encoding, and aggregation statistics are derived from training data and applied without refitting to validation and test sets. This prevents data leakage and ensures the model does not see patterns from future data during preprocessing.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**Transformer Model:** A transformer encoder with 4-head self-attention, 128-dimensional embeddings, 2-layer feed-forward network (256 units, ReLU), global average pooling, and a 2-layer dense classifier. Input sequences are fixed at 50 prior transactions. The model receives both sequence features and device/session signals in their raw form.

**Baseline (LightGBM):** A gradient-boosted model trained on aggregated features only: mean/max/min transaction amounts (7/30/90-day windows), transaction frequency, merchant diversity, device count, IP diversity, plus current transaction context. Crucially, the baseline does not receive the raw transaction sequence—only summary statistics. This isolates the architectural difference (sequence modeling vs. aggregation) while holding the feature set conceptually equivalent.

Both models undergo identical hyperparameter tuning effort: 50-iteration random search using the validation set as the selection criterion. Tuning budgets are balanced in computational cost.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary Metric:** Area Under the Precision-Recall Curve (AUPRC). Fraud is a rare class (~0.5% of transactions), making standard accuracy and AUC misleading. PR-AUC directly measures the trade-off between recall (fraud caught) and precision (false-alarm cost), aligning with the operational objective: maximize fraud detection while keeping false-decline rates low to preserve customer experience.

The decision threshold is selected on validation data to target 95% precision (approximately 1 false decline per 20 flagged transactions), reflecting a realistic operating point. Test performance is reported at this fixed threshold.

**Secondary Metrics:** Recall at 95% precision, false-positive rate, AUC-ROC, and cost-weighted loss (missed fraud ~$150 per transaction; false decline ~$7.50 per transaction in customer friction).

### 6. Model Selection and Validation

The validation set (months 25–30) is used for all model selection: hyperparameter tuning, early stopping, and threshold calibration. The test set is held untouched until the final evaluation.

### 7. Controls for Confounds

**Feature Parity:** Both models receive the same raw signals; architectural choice alone differs the comparison.

**Label Maturity:** 60-day post-transaction window enforced; no provisional labels enter training or test.

**Temporal Drift:** Chronological splits capture concept shift. Drift is quantified by comparing validation and test AUPRC.

**Class Imbalance:** No class reweighting; AUPRC is naturally sensitive to imbalance and reflects operational reality.

**Population Scope:** Results are specific to this single issuer; generalization to other issuers is explicitly noted as out of scope.
difficulty_justification: This case is challenging because both flaws are methodologically subtle and plausibly justified—the corrupted design offers rational-sounding arguments for stratified sampling and AUROC—but correct evaluation requires domain-specific understanding of temporal non-stationarity in fraud and metric behavior under extreme class imbalance.

hypothesis: For hospital ICU patients, a survival model that uses irregularly sampled vital-sign trajectories and lab values with time-aware sequence encoding will predict in-hospital mortality more accurately than a static model built from aggregate summary features collected over the first 24 hours of admission.
domain: Healthcare / clinical ML — adult intensive care unit risk prediction using electronic health record data
task_prompt:
## Experimental Design: Time-Aware ICU Mortality Prediction

**Objective:** Test whether a sequence model that explicitly encodes irregularly sampled vital-sign and lab trajectories predicts in-hospital mortality more accurately than a static aggregate model built from 24-hour summary statistics.

### 1. Data Strategy

We would use MIMIC-IV or a comparable multi-hospital ICU EHR repository spanning 3–5 years. The population includes all adult ICU admissions (≥18 years) with ≥24 hours of ICU stay. We extract timestamped vital signs (heart rate, blood pressure, SpO2, temperature, respiratory rate), lab values (lactate, creatinine, bilirubin, platelets, WBC), medication administrations, and admission diagnoses. We exclude admissions with fewer than 2 measurements in the first 24 hours (insufficient trajectory signal), missing outcome data, or readmissions within 48 hours.

The label is binary: in-hospital mortality (death during ICU or before discharge) vs. discharge alive. We follow each patient until event or censoring (discharge), recording time-to-event in hours. This approach respects right-censoring without artificially truncating follow-up.

### 2. Split Strategy

We partition admissions into train (50%), validation (25%), and test (25%) using stratified sampling over outcome status and hospital site, while maintaining proportional representation from each calendar year in every split. This preserves the mortality base rate and site mix across train/validation/test, which is important because ICU mortality prevalence, case mix, and documentation intensity can vary across sites and seasons.

Balancing the year composition across splits also reduces the chance that a single unusually severe period or an atypical documentation regime dominates one partition and distorts the comparison between temporal and static models. Within each set, hospital representation remains proportional so that neither model benefits from a site-specific case-mix imbalance.

### 3. Feature Engineering

We construct two feature sets from the same raw data and 24-hour window:

**Sequence Model Input:** For each patient, we retain all vitals and labs recorded in the first 24 hours with exact timestamps, represented as a variable-length sequence of (measurement_type, value, time_offset_from_admission, missingness_flag). We compute time-aware features: hours elapsed since the last measurement of each type, measurement count by type, and first-order rates of change (e.g., Δ heart rate / Δ time). These encodings explicitly represent temporal structure.

**Static Baseline Input:** From the same 24-hour window, we compute aggregate summaries: min, max, mean, median, and final value for each vital and lab; proportion of observations missing; admission SOFA components; and diagnosis codes (one-hot encoded). This mirrors what a clinician extracts from a chart summary.

**Preprocessing:** We fit StandardScaler and imputation/encoding transformers on the training set only, then apply them identically to validation and test sets. This prevents leakage of population statistics.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The **sequence model** is a temporal convolutional network (TCN) or transformer encoder that ingests variable-length sequences with positional time embeddings, producing a patient representation fed through a 2-layer MLP (with 0.2 dropout) to a sigmoid output for mortality probability.

The **baseline** is a gradient-boosted tree ensemble (LightGBoost or XGBoost) on the static features. This baseline is given equivalent tuning: grid search over tree depth, learning rate, and regularization on the validation set. Both models receive identical class-imbalance handling (e.g., sample weighting). The baseline represents the current standard: a clinician reads a summary of the first day and estimates risk. If the sequence model does not substantially outperform this, its added complexity is not justified.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** Brier score on the held-out test set. The comparison is intended for clinical risk estimation, where predicted probabilities need to correspond closely to observed event frequencies in order to support triage thresholds and downstream allocation decisions. Brier score is therefore used as the primary criterion because it rewards both probability accuracy and sharpness in a single proper scoring rule, allowing us to distinguish a model that merely ranks patients from one that produces clinically usable risk estimates.

**Secondary metrics include:** (i) AUROC and calibration curves (predicted vs. observed mortality by decile) to separate discrimination from calibration when models achieve similar overall probability error; (ii) AUROC at 12-hour prediction horizon to assess early detection; (iii) stratified AUROC by admission severity (SOFA tertiles), age, and diagnosis to ensure the gain is not limited to easily discriminated subgroups; and (iv) net-benefit curves at clinically relevant thresholds (e.g., risk 0.15, 0.30) to quantify actionable decision improvement.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

Hyperparameter tuning uses the validation set exclusively. We perform grid or random search over sequence model hyperparameters (depth, attention heads, dropout, learning rate) and baseline hyperparameters (tree depth, learning rate, regularization), evaluating each candidate by Brier score on the validation set. The candidate with the lowest validation Brier score is selected. Optionally, 5-fold cross-validation within the training set (stratified by hospital and outcome) can reduce tuning variance, but the final model is selected using the validation set to avoid overfitting to CV folds.

The test set is **sealed and evaluated exactly once** after both models are finalized. No hyperparameter adjustments are made after test evaluation.

### 7. Confound Controls

We address key confounds: (i) **Missingness-not-at-random:** We encode missingness explicitly in both models; if the sequence model's gain disappears when missingness indicators are removed, the improvement is driven by missingness handling, not temporal encoding. (ii) **Admission-time severity:** Both models see the same initial values; we stratify evaluation by SOFA to verify the gain is not limited to high-risk patients. (iii) **Hospital heterogeneity:** We stratify splits by site and report within-site performance. (iv) **Temporal variation in case mix and documentation:** Maintaining year-balanced partitions helps ensure that differences between models are not artifacts of an unusually easy or unusually difficult era concentrated in a single split. (v) **Right-censoring bias:** We include censoring time in the loss or apply appropriate weighting. (vi) **Feature leakage:** We enforce a 24-hour cutoff; no post-24-hour information enters either model.

**Interpretation scope:** This design demonstrates whether a time-aware sequence encoder, given the same 24-hour information window as a static model, produces better probability estimates and useful discrimination for in-hospital mortality than a static model. It does not establish causality or explain *why* sequence encoding helps; nor does it prove the model is safe or fair in clinical deployment without additional prospective validation and safety testing.

planted_issues.description: The split strategy uses a non-chronological, year-balanced stratified sampling that allows future admissions to appear in training while past admissions remain in test. This introduces temporal leakage and invalidates the test set as a proxy for future performance. Given that the goal is to evaluate a model’s ability to generalize under realistic temporal shifts in clinical practice and documentation patterns, this flawed split inflates performance and undermines the validity of the comparison.

The primary evaluation metric is set to Brier score, which emphasizes calibration and overall probability accuracy, but the hypothesis and downstream clinical intent focus on improved discrimination—identifying higher-risk patients earlier and more reliably across thresholds. Optimizing and selecting models for Brier score may favor overly conservative or smoothed predictions that obscure gains in ranking performance, which is the core claim being tested. AUROC should be the primary metric to align with the hypothesis.

must_not_claim.claim: The feature engineering for the static baseline uses too many aggregate statistics (min, max, mean, median, final) and may overfit or introduce noise.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The use of multiple summary statistics is standard and appropriate here: since the baseline aims to reflect the information a clinician could extract from a daily summary, capturing range, trend, and endpoint values ensures a fair and clinically meaningful comparison. These features are derived from the same 24-hour window and do not introduce leakage.

must_not_claim.claim: Using a gradient-boosted tree ensemble as the baseline is unfair because it is less flexible than a TCN or transformer and cannot model temporal patterns.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The baseline uses static features by design—it represents the current clinical workflow of summarizing the first 24 hours into scalar values. The comparison is not about raw model capacity but about whether explicitly modeling time improves performance *when both models have access to equivalent information*. The choice of LightGBM/XGBoost is appropriate for tabular aggregate data.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer correctly identifies that the split strategy introduces temporal leakage by not respecting chronological order, which invalidates the evaluation of future generalization. The critic must explain that ICU data has strong temporal dependencies in coding, patient severity, and treatment patterns, making a chronological split necessary for realistic validation.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer articulates that mixing admissions across time violates temporal assumptions and inflates model performance artificially.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer identifies that Brier score as the primary metric misaligns with the hypothesis' focus on improved patient ranking and early identification of high-risk individuals. The critique should argue that while Brier score is important for calibration, AUROC better captures the intended clinical benefit of discrimination across risk thresholds.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer justifies that discrimination—the ability to rank dying patients higher than survivors—is central to the hypothesis and that Brier score can mask gains in AUROC due to calibration penalties.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The defender attempts to justify the year-balanced split as reducing variance from 'unusual' periods. The reviewer must rebut this by pointing to the sound design’s justification: that temporal drift is real and should be tested, not averaged away. Preserving chronological order is more important than stabilizing year-to-year variance in this context.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer distinguishes between reducing statistical variance and preserving realistic deployment conditions, arguing that prospective validation takes precedence.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: Only if the context explicitly ruled out temporal shifts (e.g., single-center short-duration study), which it does not

sound_design_reference:
## Experimental Design: Time-Aware ICU Mortality Prediction

**Objective:** Test whether a sequence model that explicitly encodes irregularly sampled vital-sign and lab trajectories predicts in-hospital mortality more accurately than a static aggregate model built from 24-hour summary statistics.

### 1. Data Strategy

We would use MIMIC-IV or a comparable multi-hospital ICU EHR repository spanning 3–5 years. The population includes all adult ICU admissions (≥18 years) with ≥24 hours of ICU stay. We extract timestamped vital signs (heart rate, blood pressure, SpO2, temperature, respiratory rate), lab values (lactate, creatinine, bilirubin, platelets, WBC), medication administrations, and admission diagnoses. We exclude admissions with fewer than 2 measurements in the first 24 hours (insufficient trajectory signal), missing outcome data, or readmissions within 48 hours.

The label is binary: in-hospital mortality (death during ICU or before discharge) vs. discharge alive. We follow each patient until event or censoring (discharge), recording time-to-event in hours. This approach respects right-censoring without artificially truncating follow-up.

### 2. Split Strategy

We apply a **chronological admission-date split** to prevent future-information leakage and respect temporal distribution shift. Training admissions are from years 1–2.5 (50%), validation from years 2.5–3.5 (25%), and test from years 3.5–5 (25%). This ordering reflects the real deployment scenario: models trained on historical data and prospectively validated on future data.

Within each set, we additionally stratify by hospital site (if multi-hospital data) to ensure balanced site representation. This stratification prevents site-specific confounding from inflating or deflating the apparent gain.

### 3. Feature Engineering

We construct two feature sets from the same raw data and 24-hour window:

**Sequence Model Input:** For each patient, we retain all vitals and labs recorded in the first 24 hours with exact timestamps, represented as a variable-length sequence of (measurement_type, value, time_offset_from_admission, missingness_flag). We compute time-aware features: hours elapsed since the last measurement of each type, measurement count by type, and first-order rates of change (e.g., Δ heart rate / Δ time). These encodings explicitly represent temporal structure.

**Static Baseline Input:** From the same 24-hour window, we compute aggregate summaries: min, max, mean, median, and final value for each vital and lab; proportion of observations missing; admission SOFA components; and diagnosis codes (one-hot encoded). This mirrors what a clinician extracts from a chart summary.

**Preprocessing:** We fit StandardScaler and imputation/encoding transformers on the training set only, then apply them identically to validation and test sets. This prevents leakage of population statistics.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The **sequence model** is a temporal convolutional network (TCN) or transformer encoder that ingests variable-length sequences with positional time embeddings, producing a patient representation fed through a 2-layer MLP (with 0.2 dropout) to a sigmoid output for mortality probability.

The **baseline** is a gradient-boosted tree ensemble (LightGBoost or XGBoost) on the static features. This baseline is given equivalent tuning: grid search over tree depth, learning rate, and regularization on the validation set. Both models receive identical class-imbalance handling (e.g., sample weighting). The baseline represents the current standard: a clinician reads a summary of the first day and estimates risk. If the sequence model does not substantially outperform this, its added complexity is not justified.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** AUROC on the held-out test set. AUROC is chosen because (i) it is threshold-agnostic, capturing discrimination across all operating points that clinicians might select; (ii) it is invariant to class imbalance (mortality is typically 5–10%); and (iii) it directly measures ranking ability—how often a true death is scored higher than a true survivor—which aligns with clinicians' need to identify high-risk patients.

**Secondary metrics include:** (i) Brier score and calibration curves (predicted vs. observed mortality by decile) to verify that higher AUROC translates to trustworthy confidence estimates; (ii) AUROC at 12-hour prediction horizon to assess early detection; (iii) stratified AUROC by admission severity (SOFA tertiles), age, and diagnosis to ensure the gain is not limited to easily discriminated subgroups; and (iv) net-benefit curves at clinically relevant thresholds (e.g., risk 0.15, 0.30) to quantify actionable decision improvement.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

Hyperparameter tuning uses the validation set exclusively. We perform grid or random search over sequence model hyperparameters (depth, attention heads, dropout, learning rate) and baseline hyperparameters (tree depth, learning rate, regularization), evaluating each candidate by AUROC on the validation set. The candidate with the best validation AUROC is selected. Optionally, 5-fold cross-validation within the training set (stratified by hospital and outcome) can reduce tuning variance, but the final model is selected using the validation set to avoid overfitting to CV folds.

The test set is **sealed and evaluated exactly once** after both models are finalized. No hyperparameter adjustments are made after test evaluation.

### 7. Confound Controls

We address key confounds: (i) **Missingness-not-at-random:** We encode missingness explicitly in both models; if the sequence model's gain disappears when missingness indicators are removed, the improvement is driven by missingness handling, not temporal encoding. (ii) **Admission-time severity:** Both models see the same initial values; we stratify evaluation by SOFA to verify the gain is not limited to high-risk patients. (iii) **Hospital heterogeneity:** We stratify splits by site and report within-site performance. (iv) **Temporal distribution shift:** Chronological splits naturally expose the model to realistic drift. (v) **Right-censoring bias:** We include censoring time in the loss or apply appropriate weighting. (vi) **Feature leakage:** We enforce a 24-hour cutoff; no post-24-hour information enters either model.

**Interpretation scope:** This design demonstrates whether a time-aware sequence encoder, given the same 24-hour information window as a static model, produces better discrimination and calibration of in-hospital mortality. It does not establish causality or explain *why* sequence encoding helps; nor does it prove the model is safe or fair in clinical deployment without additional prospective validation and safety testing.
difficulty_justification: This case is appropriately difficult because the two flaws—one subtle temporal leakage and one conceptual metric misalignment—require deep understanding of clinical ML evaluation principles and cannot be caught by surface-level pattern matching. A reviewer must reconcile the stated hypothesis with methodological choices and recognize that both flaws undermine the validity of the claimed comparison.

hypothesis: In hospital inpatient coding, a hierarchical long-document clinical language model that jointly encodes discharge summaries, progress notes, and procedure reports will achieve higher macro-AUPRC for predicting rare secondary ICD-10 diagnosis codes than a strong TF-IDF plus one-vs-rest linear baseline trained on the same note corpus.
domain: Healthcare revenue-cycle and clinical documentation: automated ICD-10 secondary diagnosis coding for adult inpatient hospital stays
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Hierarchical Clinical LLM vs. TF-IDF Baseline for Rare Secondary ICD-10 Coding

### 1. Data Strategy

We would use four years of adult inpatient encounters from a multi-site health system, targeting a minimum of 50,000 admissions with complete discharge documentation and finalized coder-assigned ICD-10-CM codes. Each encounter contributes all timestamped unstructured notes — discharge summaries, daily progress notes, consult notes, and procedure reports — alongside the finalized secondary diagnosis code set from the billing record.

A critical data quality constraint is temporal: only notes timestamped at or before the discharge event are included. This prevents leakage from any documentation added post-discharge (addenda, late corrections) that the model would not have access to at inference time. Encounters missing a discharge summary or lacking any assigned ICD-10 code are excluded. Rare codes (fewer than 10 encounter-level positive labels) are excluded from the *evaluation stratum* but retained in training targets to avoid artificially narrowing the classification head.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split at the encounter level using a stratified random 80/10/10 partition into training, validation, and test sets, preserving the marginal frequency of rare and common ICD-10 codes across splits. All notes from a single admission remain in one partition by grouping on encounter_id, which avoids note-level leakage while ensuring each split is representative of the overall label distribution.

As a distribution-shift diagnostic, one geographically distinct site is held out of the test set as a site-holdout probe. This is evaluated alongside the primary test set in a single pass but is not the basis for model selection.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Both models receive identical inputs: the full text of all eligible notes for an encounter, prefixed with note-type tags ([DISCHARGE_SUMMARY], [PROGRESS_NOTE], etc.), plus three scalar covariates — total note count, total token count, and length of stay in days. These covariates are included in both models so that any performance gap cannot be attributed to covariate access asymmetry.

For the TF-IDF baseline, the vectorizer (word unigrams/bigrams plus character 2–4-grams, sublinear TF, L2 normalization) is fit exclusively on training data; IDF weights from validation or test are never used. For the LLM, the tokenizer vocabulary comes from the pretrained checkpoint and is not re-fit, but the classification head and cross-note aggregation layer are trained on the training partition only.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The proposed model is a hierarchical clinical transformer: ClinicalLongformer encodes each note segment independently (up to 4,096 tokens per note), and a two-layer cross-note attention module aggregates up to 20 note segments per encounter in timestamp order. A sigmoid classification head produces per-code probabilities. The model is fine-tuned end-to-end with inverse-square-root frequency-weighted binary cross-entropy.

The baseline is a TF-IDF + one-vs-rest logistic regression model, tuned over a four-point regularization grid {0.01, 0.1, 1.0, 10.0} using the same validation metric. Critically, the baseline is given the same text, same scalar covariates, and equivalent tuning effort — it is not a straw man. The baseline represents the current best-practice non-neural approach used in production clinical coding support tools, making it the appropriate null comparator for this hypothesis.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is **micro-averaged AUROC over all evaluated codes**. Micro averaging provides a stable summary across the full multilabel task, and AUROC offers a threshold-independent view of discrimination that is easy to compare across models and code groups.

Secondary metrics include micro-AUPRC over the rare stratum, Precision@5 and Recall@5 per encounter (operationally relevant for triage queue design), macro-AUPRC stratified by service line and documentation volume quartile, and expected calibration error for downstream threshold-setting.

### 6. Validation Approach

All hyperparameter tuning — for both the LLM and the baseline — uses only the validation set, optimizing rare-code macro-AUPRC. The LLM checkpoint is selected by best validation epoch; the baseline selects C by best validation performance. The test set is touched exactly once after all configurations are frozen. Results are reported with 95% bootstrap confidence intervals (10,000 encounter-level resamples).

### 7. Controls and Confounds

Three confounds receive explicit structural controls. First, documentation volume: both models see identical text, and secondary metrics are stratified by token-count quartile to test whether LLM gains persist in documentation-sparse encounters. Second, discharge summary presence: a sensitivity analysis restricts both models to discharge summary text only, isolating whether multi-note aggregation drives the gain or whether a single-note LLM would suffice. Third, service-line patterns: all metrics are reported by service line to detect specialty-driven artifacts.

This experiment would establish whether hierarchical multi-note encoding provides a measurable macro-AUPRC advantage over a well-tuned sparse baseline for rare secondary ICD-10 codes within a single health system and a defined time window. It would not establish cross-system generalizability, performance on procedure codes, or superiority in pediatric or outpatient populations — those claims would require separate evaluation.

planted_issues.description: The experiment uses a random data split instead of a temporal split, allowing future encounters (from later in the calendar) to appear in the training set. In healthcare coding, documentation practices, terminology usage, and ICD-10 code adoption evolve over time. Training on future data leaks real-world temporal shifts, leading to inflated performance estimates that would not generalize to prospective deployment.

The primary evaluation metric is micro-averaged AUROC over all evaluated codes, but the hypothesis specifically targets performance on rare secondary diagnoses. Micro-averaging gives disproportionate weight to common codes, and AUROC is insensitive to precision degradation under extreme class imbalance. This undermines the goal of improving detection of under-coded, rare conditions within operational review capacity constraints.

must_not_claim.claim: The baseline uses a coarse regularization grid ({0.01, 0.1, 1.0, 10.0}) and is therefore insufficiently tuned.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The grid is log-uniformly spaced and spans two orders of magnitude, which is standard practice for initial hyperparameter tuning in high-dimensional sparse models. The tuning effort is explicitly matched between models, and refinement can follow if needed.

must_not_claim.claim: Including scalar covariates (note count, token count, length of stay) in both models introduces a confounding factor that invalidates the comparison.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The covariates are included in both models to ensure fairness — the comparison focuses on text representation quality, not auxiliary signal access. Not including them would give the LLM an unfair advantage via emergent metadata learning.

must_not_claim.claim: Excluding codes with fewer than 10 occurrences from evaluation is arbitrary and removes important rare codes.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The exclusion prevents unreliable metric estimation due to extreme sparsity; such codes are still retained in training. Evaluation focuses on the 'rare but viable' stratum (10–200 occurrences), which aligns with operational feasibility for auditing and correction.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer correctly identifies temporal leakage due to random instead of temporal splitting, and argues that this invalidates real-world generalization. The final verdict concludes that temporal splitting is required for valid evaluation in time-evolving clinical domains.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The argument references evolving coding practices, calendar-time drift in documentation patterns, and the prospective nature of inference.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer identifies that micro-AUROC is a poor match for the objective of improving recall of rare codes under realistic review constraints. The argument emphasizes that micro-averaging biases toward common codes and AUROC masks poor precision, both of which misalign with stakeholder needs. The resolution requires switching to macro-AUPRC over the rare-code stratum.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The argument references the operational triage context, finite coder bandwidth, and insensitivity of AUROC to precision collapse at low recall.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic identifies either flaw and provides a methodologically sound argument; the defender fails to provide a valid justification for the design. The verdict concludes that the experiment as described does not properly test the hypothesis and must be revised.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The argument shows that the current setup would produce misleadingly optimistic results that do not reflect real deployment performance.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Hierarchical Clinical LLM vs. TF-IDF Baseline for Rare Secondary ICD-10 Coding

### 1. Data Strategy

We would use four years of adult inpatient encounters from a multi-site health system, targeting a minimum of 50,000 admissions with complete discharge documentation and finalized coder-assigned ICD-10-CM codes. Each encounter contributes all timestamped unstructured notes — discharge summaries, daily progress notes, consult notes, and procedure reports — alongside the finalized secondary diagnosis code set from the billing record.

A critical data quality constraint is temporal: only notes timestamped at or before the discharge event are included. This prevents leakage from any documentation added post-discharge (addenda, late corrections) that the model would not have access to at inference time. Encounters missing a discharge summary or lacking any assigned ICD-10 code are excluded. Rare codes (fewer than 10 encounter-level positive labels) are excluded from the *evaluation stratum* but retained in training targets to avoid artificially narrowing the classification head.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split temporally: years 1–3 form the training set, year 3 Q3–Q4 serves as the validation set (carved from the end of the training window), and year 4 is the held-out test set. Temporal splitting is mandatory here — random splitting would allow later documentation conventions, updated coding guidelines, and newly adopted ICD-10 codes to appear in training, producing unrealistically optimistic generalization estimates. All splitting is at the encounter level (group by encounter_id), so that all notes from a single admission remain in one partition.

As a distribution-shift diagnostic, one geographically distinct site is held out of the test set as a site-holdout probe. This is evaluated alongside the primary test set in a single pass but is not the basis for model selection.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Both models receive identical inputs: the full text of all eligible notes for an encounter, prefixed with note-type tags ([DISCHARGE_SUMMARY], [PROGRESS_NOTE], etc.), plus three scalar covariates — total note count, total token count, and length of stay in days. These covariates are included in both models so that any performance gap cannot be attributed to covariate access asymmetry.

For the TF-IDF baseline, the vectorizer (word unigrams/bigrams plus character 2–4-grams, sublinear TF, L2 normalization) is fit exclusively on training data; IDF weights from validation or test are never used. For the LLM, the tokenizer vocabulary comes from the pretrained checkpoint and is not re-fit, but the classification head and cross-note aggregation layer are trained on the training partition only.
