### 4. Models and Baseline

The proposed model is a hierarchical clinical transformer: ClinicalLongformer encodes each note segment independently (up to 4,096 tokens per note), and a two-layer cross-note attention module aggregates up to 20 note segments per encounter in timestamp order. A sigmoid classification head produces per-code probabilities. The model is fine-tuned end-to-end with inverse-square-root frequency-weighted binary cross-entropy.

The baseline is a TF-IDF + one-vs-rest logistic regression model, tuned over a four-point regularization grid {0.01, 0.1, 1.0, 10.0} using the same validation metric. Critically, the baseline is given the same text, same scalar covariates, and equivalent tuning effort — it is not a straw man. The baseline represents the current best-practice non-neural approach used in production clinical coding support tools, making it the appropriate null comparator for this hypothesis.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is **macro-AUPRC over the rare-code stratum** (codes with 10–200 training-set positive encounters). Macro averaging weights each rare code equally, directly matching the stakeholder objective of recovering under-coded secondary diagnoses regardless of their individual frequency. AUPRC is chosen over AUROC because at extreme label sparsity, AUROC is insensitive to precision collapse — a property that matters given finite coding-team review capacity.

Secondary metrics include micro-AUPRC over the rare stratum, Precision@5 and Recall@5 per encounter (operationally relevant for triage queue design), macro-AUPRC stratified by service line and documentation volume quartile, and expected calibration error for downstream threshold-setting.

### 6. Validation Approach

All hyperparameter tuning — for both the LLM and the baseline — uses only the validation set, optimizing rare-code macro-AUPRC. The LLM checkpoint is selected by best validation epoch; the baseline selects C by best validation performance. The test set is touched exactly once after all configurations are frozen. Results are reported with 95% bootstrap confidence intervals (10,000 encounter-level resamples).

### 7. Controls and Confounds

Three confounds receive explicit structural controls. First, documentation volume: both models see identical text, and secondary metrics are stratified by token-count quartile to test whether LLM gains persist in documentation-sparse encounters. Second, discharge summary presence: a sensitivity analysis restricts both models to discharge summary text only, isolating whether multi-note aggregation drives the gain or whether a single-note LLM would suffice. Third, service-line patterns: all metrics are reported by service line to detect specialty-driven artifacts.

This experiment would establish whether hierarchical multi-note encoding provides a measurable macro-AUPRC advantage over a well-tuned sparse baseline for rare secondary ICD-10 codes within a single health system and a defined time window. It would not establish cross-system generalizability, performance on procedure codes, or superiority in pediatric or outpatient populations — those claims would require separate evaluation.
difficulty_justification: This case is appropriately challenging because the two corruptions are conceptually independent—one procedural (temporal leakage), one objective-driven (metric mismatch)—and require different forms of domain insight to catch. The temporal split issue is moderately detectable but requires awareness of time drift in clinical coding, while the metric mismatch is subtle and demands understanding of stakeholder priorities around rare-code retrieval under bandwidth constraints.

hypothesis: For hospital inpatient admissions, a gradient-boosted model that uses structured EHR features plus time-aware laboratory trends will predict 48-hour ICU transfer better than a baseline logistic regression using only the latest static vitals and demographics, as measured by out-of-time AUROC and calibration.
domain: Acute inpatient clinical prediction from electronic health records in a multi-hospital health system
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: ICU Transfer Prediction — XGBoost with Time-Aware Features vs. Logistic Regression Baseline

### 1. Data Strategy

We would use retrospective EHR data spanning four calendar years (2019–2022) from a multi-hospital health system. This period is long enough to capture seasonal and practice-level variation while remaining recent enough to reflect current clinical workflows. The cohort includes all adult admissions (≥18 years) with length of stay ≥24 hours; we would expect 50,000–100,000 eligible admissions across ≥4 hospital sites.

Raw signals are extracted from the EHR: demographics (age, sex, admission type), vital signs recorded every 4–12 hours (HR, BP, RR, SpO2, temperature), time-stamped laboratory results (CBC, CMP, lactate, troponin), medication orders, diagnoses coded at admission (ICD-10), and nursing observations. The ICU transfer label is derived from bed movement audit logs, defined as a room type change from non-ICU to ICU (or equivalent monitored setting) within 24–48 hours post-admission.

Feature engineering uses an early-admission observation window anchored on the first hospital day, and the prediction target is ICU transfer occurring between hours 24 and 48. This framing is intended to reflect how deterioration risk would be summarized in practice near the day-1/day-2 handoff. We exclude patients admitted directly to ICU, those transferred out of system before hour 48, and those with >40% missing vitals or labs in the first 24 hours.

Known data quality issues—lab order patterns varying by site, documentation intensity differences, capacity-driven transfer policies—are managed through stratified splits and site-level performance evaluation (see Section 6).

### 2. Split Strategy

We employ a temporal (chronological) split stratified by hospital site. The training set comprises Year 1 data (~40,000 admissions), the validation set Year 2 data (~10,000 admissions), and the held-out test set Years 3–4 data (~20,000 admissions).

This temporal ordering is essential: it prevents the model from learning future laboratory trends or practice changes that occur across calendar years. For example, a new sepsis protocol or EHR update in 2021 would no longer leak into the training set if we train on 2019 data. Within each yearly cohort, we stratify by hospital site to ensure balanced site representation in train/val/test and to avoid a single dominant hospital driving the model. This stratification also enables later per-site evaluation to detect confounding by hospital-specific policies.

No leakage occurs because (1) the temporal order is preserved (future test data never inform training), (2) preprocessing is fit on train only (detailed in Section 3), and (3) the feature set is assembled from information available during the early inpatient course rather than from downstream outcome coding.

### 3. Preprocessing and Features

All preprocessing transformers—imputation, scaling, categorical encoding—are fit exclusively on the training set and applied without re-fitting to validation and test sets. This mimics deployment: the model sees new patients whose distributions may differ slightly from the training cohort.

Missing laboratory values are imputed within the feature window using mean imputation (fit on training data). Continuous vitals and labs are standardized via z-score scaling (training set mean and SD). Categorical variables (admission type, diagnosis groups) are one-hot encoded according to the vocabulary in training data; unseen categories in val or test are mapped to 'unknown'.

Final features include: (1) **Static features**: age, sex, admission type, Charlson comorbidity index (from ICD-10), admission source. (2) **Time-aware dynamic features**: for each vital (HR, BP, RR, SpO2, temp), we compute mean, min, max, and linear trend slope over the early-admission trajectory; abnormal lab flags (lactate >2, creatinine >1.5× baseline, platelets <100K, hemoglobin <7); medication flags (vasopressor use, antibiotics within the first hospital day); nursing alerts (rapid response calls, code status changes); and the ward acuity score already maintained in the nursing workflow and refreshed daily in the operational record.

Including the ward acuity score is useful because it summarizes cross-signal deterioration patterns that may not be fully represented by individual measurements, while remaining aligned with the information clinicians review at the bedside. Features are finalized using the patient state available by the hospital day-1/day-2 handoff so that the model consumes the same condensed status representation that would support escalation decisions in practice.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The **proposed model** is a gradient-boosted tree ensemble (XGBoost or LightGBM). Hyperparameters are tuned via random search on the validation set: tree depth 5–7, learning rate in [0.01, 0.1], early stopping on validation Brier score with patience = 10 rounds. The model outputs probability scores.

The **baseline** is logistic regression using only the latest static vitals and demographics: age, sex, most recent HR, systolic/diastolic BP, RR, SpO2, temperature within 24 hours, and latest available creatinine and hemoglobin. This represents the simpler alternative a clinician might already use. Logistic regression is fit with L2 regularization; regularization strength is tuned via grid search on the validation set using the same Brier score metric.

Both models receive equivalent tuning effort and are evaluated under identical conditions. The baseline is appropriate because it isolates the hypothesis claim: that gradient boosting *plus* time-aware trends outperforms a static logistic regression baseline.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric**: Out-of-time calibration error on the held-out test set (Years 3–4 data), summarized with the Brier score.

Brier score is appropriate because the intended use is triage support rather than pure case finding: a model that outputs well-scaled absolute risk is more useful for setting escalation thresholds, comparing risk across wards, and monitoring changes in alert burden over time than one optimized only for pairwise ranking. Using a held-out time period ensures the comparison reflects real-world generalization rather than adaptation to a single evaluation window.

**Secondary metrics**:
- **Calibration** (Hosmer–Lemeshow test, calibration slope on test set): because risk scores may be used to trigger interventions, predicted probabilities must align with observed event rates.
- **Sensitivity at 90% specificity and specificity at 90% sensitivity**: clinically meaningful operating points that illustrate the sensitivity/specificity trade-off and help stakeholders choose a deployment threshold.
- **Per-hospital Brier score on the test set**: stratified by site to check whether gains hold uniformly across hospitals and to detect whether improvements reflect hospital-specific ordering patterns rather than true physiologic signal.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

Hyperparameters for both the proposed model and baseline are selected using the validation set (Year 2 data) based on validation Brier score. Early stopping in the boosting model is triggered by lack of improvement in validation Brier score. The test set is reserved for final evaluation only and is not touched during any tuning iterations.

This three-way split (train/val/test) prevents overfitting to the test set and yields an honest estimate of generalization. No iteration or tweaking occurs on the test set; results are reported once, with 95% confidence intervals and paired significance testing for the primary metric where appropriate.

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

To address concerns that ICU transfer reflects hospital policy and workflow rather than patient physiology, we implement the following controls:

1. **Per-site analysis**: calibration and primary-metric performance are computed separately for each hospital in the test set. If the proposed model's advantage is driven by a single site or if a site shows degraded performance, that suggests overfitting to hospital-specific patterns.

2. **Temporal stability**: the primary metric is computed separately for Q1 and Q2 of the test period (Years 3–4 split in half) to check whether gains are stable or decay, which would suggest the model learned time-sensitive practice changes.

3. **Confound measurement**: we record ICU transfer rates by site and year across train/val/test; if transfer rates are uncorrelated with administrative data on hospital capacity or staffing, this weakens the confounding hypothesis.

4. **Feature importance analysis**: we examine which features most influence predictions in the proposed model; if top features are site-specific (e.g., particular antibiotics used at one hospital), this flags potential confounding.

**What this experiment establishes**: That a gradient-boosted model using time-aware features yields better out-of-time risk estimation for ICU transfer than logistic regression on static vitals and demographics, at least in the health system and time period studied.

**What it does not establish**: Causality; whether the model will generalize to other health systems; whether the label truly reflects physiologic deterioration vs. administrative or capacity-driven decisions (though per-site and temporal analyses help bound this concern).

A fair critic would scrutinize per-site performance, temporal stability, and feature importance closely.

planted_issues.description: The inclusion of a daily-updated ward acuity score as a feature risks target leakage because this score may incorporate nursing assessments or interventions that occur after hour 24, overlapping with the 24–48 hour prediction window for ICU transfer. Although intended to reflect a clinician-usable summary, the score could indirectly encode downstream clinical decisions that correlate strongly with ICU transfer, enabling the model to exploit post-hoc justification rather than prospectively capture physiological deterioration.

Using Brier score as the primary metric misaligns with the stated goal of improving ICU transfer prediction via time-aware features. Brier score emphasizes calibration and probability fidelity, but the hypothesis focuses on model discrimination (i.e., ranking patients by risk). A model with poor discrimination but conservative probability estimates could outperform a better-ranking model on Brier score, especially under class imbalance. The hypothesis specifically invokes predictive performance in triage contexts where ranking patients matters more than exact risk calibration.

must_not_claim.claim: Using a temporal split introduces bias because calendar years may not represent natural fold boundaries.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Temporal splits are essential in clinical forecasting to prevent leakage from future practice trends (e.g., protocol changes, EHR updates), and are appropriate here to ensure the model generalizes to future patient populations.

must_not_claim.claim: The use of mean imputation for missing labs is inappropriate due to data sparsity.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Mean imputation is acceptable when applied using training-set statistics, especially in settings with moderate missingness; the design explicitly fits imputation on training data only, so this does not constitute leakage or poor practice.

must_not_claim.claim: A model comparison should use multiple performance metrics to avoid narrow optimization.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The design appropriately includes secondary metrics for calibration and operating points; the concern arises only if the *primary* metric misaligns with the hypothesis, which is a separate issue.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic identifies that the ward acuity score may incorporate post-hour-24 assessments, potentially leaking information from the target window. The defender fails to provide a timestamped protocol for feature cutoffs earlier than hour 24, making this a substantive leakage risk.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic demonstrates that the ward acuity score is updated after hour 24 and correlates with ICU transfer beyond what baseline features explain.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic argues that Brier score is a poor primary metric because it conflates calibration and discrimination, and does not prioritize the model's ability to rank at-risk patients — a central goal in triage. The defender fails to show that better Brier score corresponds reliably to higher AUROC or improved ranking.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic shows that a model could manipulate Brier score by outputting uniform intermediate probabilities without improving true risk ordering.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The debate concludes that the feature pipeline lacks a strict timestamp barrier before hour 24, and the evaluation prioritizes probability fidelity over ranking, undermining confidence in the model's ability to predict ICU transfer. The verdict must be 'critique'.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Both issues are substantiated: evidence shows feature cutoffs could conflict with outcome timing, and the primary metric does not emphasize discriminative performance.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: No viable defense exists for either flaw under the hypothesis.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: ICU Transfer Prediction — XGBoost with Time-Aware Features vs. Logistic Regression Baseline

### 1. Data Strategy

We would use retrospective EHR data spanning four calendar years (2019–2022) from a multi-hospital health system. This period is long enough to capture seasonal and practice-level variation while remaining recent enough to reflect current clinical workflows. The cohort includes all adult admissions (≥18 years) with length of stay ≥24 hours; we would expect 50,000–100,000 eligible admissions across ≥4 hospital sites.

Raw signals are extracted from the EHR: demographics (age, sex, admission type), vital signs recorded every 4–12 hours (HR, BP, RR, SpO2, temperature), time-stamped laboratory results (CBC, CMP, lactate, troponin), medication orders, diagnoses coded at admission (ICD-10), and nursing observations. The ICU transfer label is derived from bed movement audit logs, defined as a room type change from non-ICU to ICU (or equivalent monitored setting) within 24–48 hours post-admission.

Feature engineering uses a fixed 24-hour window from admission through hour 24; the prediction target is ICU transfer occurring between hours 24 and 48. This prospective framing ensures no leakage from future treatment decisions. We exclude patients admitted directly to ICU, those transferred out of system before hour 48, and those with >40% missing vitals or labs in the first 24 hours.

Known data quality issues—lab order patterns varying by site, documentation intensity differences, capacity-driven transfer policies—are managed through stratified splits and site-level performance evaluation (see Section 6).

### 2. Split Strategy

We employ a temporal (chronological) split stratified by hospital site. The training set comprises Year 1 data (~40,000 admissions), the validation set Year 2 data (~10,000 admissions), and the held-out test set Years 3–4 data (~20,000 admissions).

This temporal ordering is essential: it prevents the model from learning future laboratory trends or practice changes that occur across calendar years. For example, a new sepsis protocol or EHR update in 2021 would no longer leak into the training set if we train on 2019 data. Within each yearly cohort, we stratify by hospital site to ensure balanced site representation in train/val/test and to avoid a single dominant hospital driving the model. This stratification also enables later per-site evaluation to detect confounding by hospital-specific policies.

No leakage occurs because (1) the temporal order is preserved (future test data never inform training), (2) preprocessing is fit on train only (detailed in Section 3), and (3) the feature window closes at hour 24, before the prediction target (hours 24–48).

### 3. Preprocessing and Features

All preprocessing transformers—imputation, scaling, categorical encoding—are fit exclusively on the training set and applied without re-fitting to validation and test sets. This mimics deployment: the model sees new patients whose distributions may differ slightly from the training cohort.

Missing laboratory values are imputed within the feature window using mean imputation (fit on training data). Continuous vitals and labs are standardized via z-score scaling (training set mean and SD). Categorical variables (admission type, diagnosis groups) are one-hot encoded according to the vocabulary in training data; unseen categories in val or test are mapped to 'unknown'.

Final features include: (1) **Static features**: age, sex, admission type, Charlson comorbidity index (from ICD-10), admission source. (2) **Time-aware dynamic features**: for each vital (HR, BP, RR, SpO2, temp), we compute mean, min, max, and linear trend slope over the 24-hour window; abnormal lab flags (lactate >2, creatinine >1.5× baseline, platelets <100K, hemoglobin <7); medication flags (vasopressor use, antibiotics within 24h); nursing alerts (rapid response calls, code status changes).

No feature encodes the outcome or contains information from the 24–48 hour window (e.g., no ICU orders or post-hour-24 therapy changes).

### 4. Model and Baseline

The **proposed model** is a gradient-boosted tree ensemble (XGBoost or LightGBM). Hyperparameters are tuned via random search on the validation set: tree depth 5–7, learning rate in [0.01, 0.1], early stopping on validation AUROC with patience = 10 rounds. The model outputs probability scores.

The **baseline** is logistic regression using only the latest static vitals and demographics: age, sex, most recent HR, systolic/diastolic BP, RR, SpO2, temperature within 24 hours, and latest available creatinine and hemoglobin. This represents the simpler alternative a clinician might already use. Logistic regression is fit with L2 regularization; regularization strength is tuned via grid search on the validation set using the same AUROC metric.

Both models receive equivalent tuning effort and are evaluated under identical conditions. The baseline is appropriate because it isolates the hypothesis claim: that gradient boosting *plus* time-aware trends outperforms a static logistic regression baseline.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric**: Out-of-time AUROC on the held-out test set (Years 3–4 data).

AUROC is appropriate because (1) it is threshold-independent and measures discrimination across all operating points, (2) it handles class imbalance naturally (ICU transfer is typically 5–15% of admissions), (3) it aligns with clinicians' need to rank-order patients by risk for resource prioritization, and (4) using a held-out time period ensures the comparison reflects real-world generalization rather than test-set fitting.

**Secondary metrics**:
- **Calibration** (Hosmer–Lemeshow test, calibration slope on test set): because risk scores may be used to trigger interventions, predicted probabilities must align with observed event rates.
- **Sensitivity at 90% specificity and specificity at 90% sensitivity**: clinically meaningful operating points that illustrate the sensitivity/specificity trade-off and help stakeholders choose a deployment threshold.
- **Per-hospital AUROC on the test set**: stratified by site to check whether gains hold uniformly across hospitals and to detect whether improvements reflect hospital-specific ordering patterns rather than true physiologic signal.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

Hyperparameters for both the proposed model and baseline are selected using the validation set (Year 2 data) based on validation AUROC. Early stopping in the boosting model is triggered by lack of improvement in validation AUROC. The test set is reserved for final evaluation only and is not touched during any tuning iterations.

This three-way split (train/val/test) prevents overfitting to the test set and yields an honest estimate of generalization. No iteration or tweaking occurs on the test set; results are reported once, with 95% confidence intervals and p-values (e.g., DeLong test for correlated AUROC estimates).

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

To address concerns that ICU transfer reflects hospital policy and workflow rather than patient physiology, we implement the following controls:

1. **Per-site analysis**: AUROC and calibration are computed separately for each hospital in the test set. If the proposed model's advantage is driven by a single site or if a site shows degraded performance, that suggests overfitting to hospital-specific patterns.

2. **Temporal stability**: AUROC is computed separately for Q1 and Q2 of the test period (Years 3–4 split in half) to check whether gains are stable or decay, which would suggest the model learned time-sensitive practice changes.

3. **Confound measurement**: we record ICU transfer rates by site and year across train/val/test; if transfer rates are uncorrelated with administrative data on hospital capacity or staffing, this weakens the confounding hypothesis.

4. **Feature importance analysis**: we examine which features most influence predictions in the proposed model; if top features are site-specific (e.g., particular antibiotics used at one hospital), this flags potential confounding.

**What this experiment establishes**: That a gradient-boosted model using time-aware features achieves better out-of-time discrimination (AUROC) for ICU transfer than logistic regression on static vitals and demographics, at least in the health system and time period studied.

**What it does not establish**: Causality; whether the model will generalize to other health systems; whether the label truly reflects physiologic deterioration vs. administrative or capacity-driven decisions (though per-site and temporal analyses help bound this concern).

A fair critic would scrutinize per-site performance, temporal stability, and feature importance closely.
difficulty_justification: This case is challenging because both corruptions are subtle: one requires tracing feature timestamp logic against prediction windows, and the other involves reasoning about metric alignment with clinical intent rather than just methodological correctness. Together, they test deep domain reasoning beyond pattern recognition.

hypothesis: A transformer-based code recommendation model that incorporates a developer’s recent file-edit sequence and repository dependency graph will rank the correct next code completion higher than a token-only language model, as measured by top-k suggestion accuracy on real pull requests.
domain: Software engineering productivity in large-scale open-source and enterprise code repositories
task_prompt:
This experiment tests whether a transformer-based code recommendation model that incorporates developer file-edit sequence and repository dependency graphs outranks a token-only language model baseline on real pull request data.

**1. Data Collection and Labeling**

We would use 24 months of version control and IDE telemetry from a curated set of open-source and enterprise repositories. The dataset includes ordered file-edit sequences with timestamps, complete diffs, commit metadata, dependency graphs (parsed from imports), and accepted code completions extracted from merged pull request diffs or IDE logs.

For each developer session, the ground-truth label is the exact code span the developer accepted and committed. A completion is considered correct if it matches the accepted span exactly (with minor whitespace tolerance defined a priori). We only include completions that were generated within 2 seconds of the cursor position, ensuring they were contextually available when the developer made their choice.

**2. Splitting Strategy**

We employ a repository-stratified session split across the full 24-month corpus. Developer sessions are partitioned into training, validation, and test sets in a 60/20/20 ratio while preserving each repository’s contribution and acceptance-rate profile in every split. This design keeps the candidate-completion mix, repository complexity, and rare-library usage comparable across splits, reducing the chance that model differences are driven by one atypical development period or by sparsity in a small subset of repositories.

**3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing**

All preprocessing and vocabulary fitting is performed exclusively on the training data. We construct two feature streams: (1) **Sequence features**: the last 10 file edits in the developer's session, represented as (file_path_embedding, diff_summary, timestamp_delta); (2) **Graph features**: the repository dependency graph at the time of completion, represented as (imported_module, importing_file) edges, updated per commit to reflect the current state without leakage. Code tokens are tokenized from training vocabulary only. Critically, we exclude hand-crafted features (e.g., library popularity, API frequency) that would confound the graph and sequence signal.

**4. Model and Baseline**

The proposed model is a transformer with two branches: a sequence encoder over file-edit history and a graph encoder over the dependency DAG. Both encoders are concatenated and fed to a ranking head that scores candidate completions via pairwise ranking loss (e.g., BPR).

The baseline is a token-only transformer decoder with identical architecture and capacity, receiving only code tokens and immediate edit history—no dependency graph or file-context. Both models receive equivalent hyperparameter tuning on the validation set (same search budget, same tuning metric) and are trained with the same ranking loss.

**5. Evaluation Metrics**

The primary metric is **mean reciprocal rank (MRR)**, computed per developer session over the scored candidate set and averaged across the test set. This captures how close the accepted completion is to the top of the ranked list and provides a stable view of ranking quality even when candidate-pool difficulty varies across repositories and coding contexts.

Secondary metrics include top-1 accuracy, top-5 accuracy, recall@10, and false positive rate to assess robustness and user experience.

**6. Model Selection and Test Policy**

Both baseline and proposed model are tuned and selected on the validation set using MRR. The test set is held until final evaluation and is used exactly once to report results. Significance is assessed via bootstrap confidence intervals and paired statistical testing (α = 0.05).

**7. Controls for Confounds**

To isolate the value of graph and sequence context from project-specific memorization, we implement four controls: (1) Repository stratification ensures broad project coverage in each split; (2) A project-blind baseline prevents the model from using repository metadata; (3) A held-out repository is withheld entirely from training and validation, tested separately to detect project overfitting; (4) Per-library analysis breaks down performance by library popularity to confirm that improvements come from context learning, not baseline coverage of popular APIs.

This design allows us to establish with high confidence whether the proposed model's ranking improvements are attributable to context incorporation or to other factors.

planted_issues.description: The splitting strategy uses a random session split across the full 24-month corpus, which allows future developer sessions and evolved repository states to appear in training data, then be evaluated in test. Since the model uses time-dependent features like file-edit sequences and dynamic dependency graphs, this introduces temporal leakage: the model could learn from future coding patterns and incorrectly appear to generalize when it is in fact memorizing non-stationary patterns. This invalidates the evaluation of prospective real-world deployment performance.

The primary evaluation metric is mean reciprocal rank (MRR), but the operational goal is whether the correct completion appears within the top few suggestions (e.g., top-5) in a real IDE UI. MRR rewards incremental improvements deeper in the ranked list that may not change user experience, and enables model selection on improvements that do not satisfy the practical success criterion. This mismatch means the model may be optimized for a statistically smooth metric rather than actual usability.

must_not_claim.claim: The 60/20/20 split ratio is arbitrary and not rigorous
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The split ratio itself is not problematic; temporal stratification and leakage prevention are more important than exact percentages in this domain. A fixed ratio is acceptable if applied correctly, but here the fundamental issue is temporal mixing, not the proportions.

must_not_claim.claim: Using MRR as a secondary metric introduces evaluation contamination
must_not_claim.why_wrong: MRR is a valid and informative secondary ranking metric. It is acceptable to report it alongside primary metrics; the flaw lies only in using it for model selection and claiming it as primary when it misaligns with the stakeholder objective.

must_not_claim.claim: Excluding hand-crafted features like library popularity harms model performance
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Such features are explicitly excluded to prevent confounding: if the model improves merely by memorizing popular APIs, it does not prove that sequence or graph context is helpful. Removing these ensures the experimental comparison isolates the value of structural context.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer successfully argues that the experiment design risks temporal leakage due to a non-temporal data split, and that this invalidates the generalization claim. The defender cannot legitimately justify a random session split in a strongly time-ordered, evolving system like code development.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic demonstrates that future sessions contribute to training while past sessions appear in test, breaking causal order and enabling information from the future to influence present predictions.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer identifies that selecting models based on MRR, while the stakeholder cares about top-k usability (e.g., top-5 correctness), creates a metric misalignment. Improvements in MRR may not translate to better user experience.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic shows that a model can improve MRR by elevating correct completions from rank 10 to 7 without ever placing them in the visible UI range (e.g., top 5), thus failing the practical goal.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The debate concludes that both flaws are unambiguously present and cannot be defended: temporal leakage invalidates generalization, and metric alignment is essential for operational validity. Therefore, a critique verdict is required.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic establishes both issues and shows that neither has a valid rebuttal given the hypothesis and stakeholder context.

sound_design_reference:
This experiment tests whether a transformer-based code recommendation model that incorporates developer file-edit sequence and repository dependency graphs outranks a token-only language model baseline on real pull request data.

**1. Data Collection and Labeling**

We would use 24 months of version control and IDE telemetry from a curated set of open-source and enterprise repositories. The dataset includes ordered file-edit sequences with timestamps, complete diffs, commit metadata, dependency graphs (parsed from imports), and accepted code completions extracted from merged pull request diffs or IDE logs.

For each developer session, the ground-truth label is the exact code span the developer accepted and committed. A completion is considered correct if it matches the accepted span exactly (with minor whitespace tolerance defined a priori). We only include completions that were generated within 2 seconds of the cursor position, ensuring they were contextually available when the developer made their choice.

**2. Splitting Strategy**

We employ a temporal split with repository stratification. The 24-month window is divided into three periods: months 1–12 (training), 13–18 (validation), 19–24 (test). Within each period, data is stratified by repository so that each repository contributes to all three splits. This design prevents temporal leakage—no future information flows into training—and avoids confounding from project-specific memorization by ensuring the model must generalize across repositories and time periods.

**3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing**

All preprocessing and vocabulary fitting is performed exclusively on the training data. We construct two feature streams: (1) **Sequence features**: the last 10 file edits in the developer's session, represented as (file_path_embedding, diff_summary, timestamp_delta); (2) **Graph features**: the repository dependency graph at the time of completion, represented as (imported_module, importing_file) edges, updated per commit to reflect the current state without leakage. Code tokens are tokenized from training vocabulary only. Critically, we exclude hand-crafted features (e.g., library popularity, API frequency) that would confound the graph and sequence signal.

**4. Model and Baseline**

The proposed model is a transformer with two branches: a sequence encoder over file-edit history and a graph encoder over the dependency DAG. Both encoders are concatenated and fed to a ranking head that scores candidate completions via pairwise ranking loss (e.g., BPR).

The baseline is a token-only transformer decoder with identical architecture and capacity, receiving only code tokens and immediate edit history—no dependency graph or file-context. Both models receive equivalent hyperparameter tuning on the validation set (same search budget, same tuning metric) and are trained with the same ranking loss.

**5. Evaluation Metrics**

The primary metric is **top-5 accuracy**: the fraction of test sessions where the correct completion ranks in the top 5 suggestions. This reflects the stakeholder's operational goal—a developer must find the correct suggestion quickly to reduce effort—and respects UI constraints (more than ~5 suggestions increase cognitive load).

Secondary metrics include top-1 accuracy, mean reciprocal rank, recall@10, and false positive rate to assess robustness and user experience.

**6. Model Selection and Test Policy**

Both baseline and proposed model are tuned and selected on the validation set (months 13–18) using top-5 accuracy. The test set is held until final evaluation and is used exactly once to report results. Significance is assessed via bootstrap confidence intervals and paired statistical testing (α = 0.05).

**7. Controls for Confounds**

To isolate the value of graph and sequence context from project-specific memorization, we implement four controls: (1) Repository stratification ensures generalization across projects; (2) A project-blind baseline prevents the model from using repository metadata; (3) A held-out repository is withheld entirely from training and validation, tested separately to detect project overfitting; (4) Per-library analysis breaks down performance by library popularity to confirm that improvements come from context learning, not baseline coverage of popular APIs.

This design allows us to establish with high confidence whether the proposed model's ranking improvements are attributable to context incorporation or to other factors.
difficulty_justification: This case is challenging because both corruptions are subtle and domain-specific: temporal leakage in code recommendation systems is easy to overlook without deep awareness of evolving repository state, and metric misalignment requires understanding both ranking theory and real-world IDE constraints. A reviewer must connect methodological flaws to operational impact, not just identify surface-level issues.

hypothesis: A graph neural network built on account-device-IP-beneficiary transfer relationships detects suspicious money-laundering transaction chains more effectively than a transaction-level gradient-boosted tree baseline using engineered tabular features, when evaluated on future SAR-linked alerts in a retail bank.
domain: Anti-money-laundering monitoring for a retail/commercial bank, where investigators review alerted transactions and accounts for potential suspicious activity reports (SARs).
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: GNN vs. GBT for AML Transaction Chain Detection

### 1. Data and Labels

The experiment uses 36 months of production transaction logs from a single retail/commercial bank. Each record includes sender and receiver account IDs, timestamp, amount, channel, device fingerprint, IP address, KYC tier, counterparty identifiers, and historical alert and investigation outcomes. Using a single institution's data is deliberate: it controls for cross-institution variation in SAR filing culture and ensures the graph topology reflects one real deployment environment.

Labels are defined at the transaction level: a transaction is positive if it belongs to a cluster where any linked account filed a SAR within 90 days of the transaction date. The 90-day forward window is enforced strictly at each split boundary — no SAR outcome from beyond the window is used, and a 30-day dead zone at each boundary absorbs in-flight investigations. Transactions with no outcome within 90 days are treated as negatives, consistent with operational convention and applied uniformly to both models.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split at the transaction level into 80% training, 10% validation, and 10% test using a stratified random split to preserve the rare positive rate across partitions. Because AML labels are highly imbalanced, stratification helps ensure stable model selection and fair comparison between the GNN and GBT across all splits.

Graph edges are constructed within each split window using only transactions whose timestamps fall within that window plus an allowed historical lookback. No test-period edges are materialized during training.

### 3. Feature Engineering

All preprocessing transformers — log-scaling, categorical encoders, imputation — are fit on the training set only and applied frozen to validation and test. Rolling aggregate features (e.g., 30-day transaction velocity, degree counts) are computed using only data available at or before the transaction timestamp, enforcing strict causal ordering.

Critically, the GBT baseline receives tabular versions of all relational signals the GNN processes: unique counterparty counts over 7/30/90-day windows, shared device and IP linkage counts, fan-out degree, and training-period betweenness centrality. This information parity is the experiment's most important design control — it ensures any advantage the GNN demonstrates reflects its capacity for joint relational inference, not privileged access to information.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The proposed model is a heterogeneous GNN (GraphSAGE or R-GCN, 2–3 layers) operating on the account-device-IP-beneficiary multigraph, producing transaction-level suspicion scores. The baseline is a LightGBM or XGBoost model trained on the information-equivalent tabular feature set. Both models receive equivalent tuning effort: a 100-configuration randomized search over their respective hyperparameter spaces, evaluated on validation AP. Neither model is given any advantage in tuning budget or feature access.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is AUROC on the test set. AUROC is a threshold-independent measure that captures overall discrimination quality and makes the results easier to compare with prior AML detection literature, while remaining robust to the choice of operating point.

Secondary metrics include precision at recall thresholds of 0.40 and 0.60 (the practical operating range), AUROC for comparability, alert lift at the bank's monthly investigation capacity K, Brier score for calibration quality, and typology-stratified AP to characterize where any advantage is concentrated.

### 6. Validation and Test Set Discipline

All model selection — architecture choices, hyperparameter configurations, early stopping — is performed using validation AP on months 25–27 only. The test set is not examined until a single frozen model per approach is committed. The test set is used exactly once. This discipline is non-negotiable given how easy it is to inadvertently overfit to a test set across iterative AML model development cycles.

### 7. Confound Controls

Three confounds are explicitly managed. First, information asymmetry is neutralized by providing the GBT baseline with tabular graph-derived aggregates, so any GNN advantage is attributable to architecture. Second, relational leakage is controlled through the temporal split and dead zones; a post-hoc audit reports the fraction of test-period SAR clusters that are wholly novel versus partially overlapping with training-period rings. Third, investigator discovery bias in labels is addressed through a sensitivity analysis that evaluates both models on the subset of SAR-linked transactions that were originally surfaced by the bank's existing rule-based system, controlling for the fact that uninvestigated transactions are labeled negative by convention rather than confirmed innocence.

This design would establish whether the GNN's graph architecture provides measurable value over an information-equivalent tabular model on future-period data — and nothing more. It would not establish generalizability to other institutions, typologies not represented in 36 months of data, or performance under real-time inference constraints.

planted_issues.description: The experiment uses a stratified random split at the transaction level, which allows transactions from the same laundering ring or entity to appear in both training and test sets. This introduces temporal and relational leakage, as future behavior is inadvertently exposed during training. Since the hypothesis explicitly concerns generalization to future patterns, the evaluation fails to assess real-world performance and risks inflating the GNN's apparent advantage due to data leakage.

The primary evaluation metric is AUROC, which is poorly suited for this task due to extreme class imbalance (SAR-positive rate < 0.5%). AUROC gives excessive weight to true negatives, which dominate the sample, and can mask poor precision in the top-ranked alerts. Since the operational goal is to maximize detection within a fixed investigation capacity, Average Precision (AP) or precision-recall curves would be far more aligned with business impact.

must_not_claim.claim: The GBT baseline should not receive network-derived aggregate features because it gives the baseline 'unfair access' to relational information.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Providing the GBT baseline with tabular versions of relational signals (e.g., counterparty counts, shared devices) is essential to ensure information parity. Without this, any GNN advantage could be attributed to privileged feature access rather than architectural superiority. The design correctly isolates the value of joint relational inference by controlling for this confound.

must_not_claim.claim: Using a single institution’s data limits generalizability and therefore invalidates the experiment.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The hypothesis is about performance in a real deployment environment, not universal generalization. Using single-institution data is appropriate and intentional: it ensures the graph topology reflects actual fraud patterns and controls for institution-specific SAR-filing practices, which vary widely.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic correctly identifies that the random transaction-level split introduces temporal and relational leakage, invalidating the future generalization claim. The defender cannot credibly justify this split for a time-sensitive, graph-structured AML task. The final verdict must be that the design is flawed and must be revised with a chronological split to be valid.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic explains that correlated entities across time can appear in both train and test, allowing leakage of future relational patterns and undermining the stated hypothesis about future-period performance.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic identifies that AUROC is misaligned with operational objectives under extreme class imbalance. A credible critique shows that high AUROC can coexist with poor precision in the top-ranked alerts, which harms real-world utility. The defender cannot override this mismatch, though could argue AUROC has value as a secondary comparability metric.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic demonstrates via example or reference that AUROC is insensitive to false positives in the high-precision regime critical for AML investigations, and that AP or precision-at-recall would better reflect queue-based prioritization.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The defender acknowledges AUROC's limitations but argues it is included to facilitate comparison with published literature; however, they must concede that AP should be the primary decision-making metric.

## Experiment Design: GNN vs. GBT for AML Transaction Chain Detection

### 1. Data and Labels

The experiment uses 36 months of production transaction logs from a single retail/commercial bank. Each record includes sender and receiver account IDs, timestamp, amount, channel, device fingerprint, IP address, KYC tier, counterparty identifiers, and historical alert and investigation outcomes. Using a single institution's data is deliberate: it controls for cross-institution variation in SAR filing culture and ensures the graph topology reflects one real deployment environment.
