hypothesis: For a large online fashion marketplace, a session-based Transformer recommender trained on ordered click/add-to-cart/purchase events will improve next-item purchase ranking compared with a matrix-factorization model built from historical user-item interactions, when evaluated on newly arriving sessions from a later time period.
domain: E-commerce fashion marketplace recommendation during live browsing sessions on web and mobile apps
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Session-Based Transformer vs. Matrix Factorization for Next-Item Purchase Ranking

### 1. Objective and Scope

The goal is to determine whether a session-based Transformer recommender, trained on ordered in-session interaction sequences, produces superior next-item purchase ranking compared with a hybrid matrix factorization model trained on historical user-item interactions. The claim is scoped to future sessions arriving after the training window — it is not a claim about all recommendation settings, but specifically about the sequential, session-aware inference context of a live fashion marketplace.

### 2. Data Collection and Labeling

We would use 18 months of production event logs containing timestamped impressions, clicks, wishlist adds, add-to-cart events, and completed purchases for all sessions on web and mobile. Product metadata (category, brand, price, discount status, inventory flag) is extracted from daily catalog snapshots. Labels are directly observed from purchase events — no annotation is required. The ranking task is defined at the session level: given the interaction prefix before a purchase event, rank the catalog such that the purchased item ranks highly. Only sessions containing at least one purchase serve as labeled evaluation examples.

### 3. Temporal Split Strategy

Data is split strictly chronologically: months 1–12 for training, months 13–15 for validation, and months 16–18 as the held-out test set. A session is assigned to whichever split contains its first event timestamp. This temporal split is non-negotiable for this domain: fashion assortments rotate seasonally, promotions recur on predictable cycles, and user intent shifts with trends. A random split would allow models to see future seasonal signals during training, producing optimistic and unrealistic estimates. Stratification by device type and session-length bucket is enforced across splits to ensure distribution comparability.

### 4. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

For the Transformer, each session is represented as an ordered sequence of (item embedding, event type, time delta, metadata embeddings) tuples, capped at 50 events. For LightFM, the same item metadata features are passed as item side features in the hybrid formulation — this is the critical design decision ensuring both models have identical informational access to catalog attributes. All preprocessing (vocabulary construction, price normalization, frequency filters) is fit on the training partition only and applied to validation and test without refitting, preventing any leakage of future distribution statistics.

### 5. Models and Baseline

The proposed model is a SASRec-style 2-layer, 4-head causal Transformer trained with sampled softmax loss. The baseline is LightFM with identical item metadata side features — chosen specifically because it handles cold-start items through metadata embeddings, giving it structural parity with the Transformer on the cold-start dimension. Both models receive 50 hyperparameter search trials evaluated on validation NDCG@10. Anonymous sessions are handled identically by both models via a shared popularity-plus-category-affinity fallback.

### 6. Evaluation and Metrics

The primary metric is NDCG@10, computed against the full item catalog per session and averaged across all test sessions. NDCG@10 aligns with the stakeholder's goal of surfacing purchased items within the visible recommendation surface and rewards higher placements proportionally. Secondary metrics include HR@10, MRR, catalog Coverage@10 (to monitor fresh-item visibility), and stratified NDCG@10 by session length, device type, and cold-start status. Significance is assessed via paired Wilcoxon signed-rank test (α=0.05, Bonferroni-corrected).

### 7. Confound Controls

Three confounds receive explicit design controls. First, feature parity: LightFM's hybrid mode ensures the MF baseline has access to the same metadata, isolating sequence modeling as the independent variable. Second, catalog availability: both models use the same daily inventory snapshot at inference time, and cold-start NDCG@10 is reported separately. Third, popularity bias: metrics are computed against the full catalog rather than a sampled negative set, preventing well-known popularity-sampling artifacts from inflating scores. Results are further stratified by position in the test window to detect differential seasonal drift between models.

A positive result — statistically significant improvement in NDCG@10 on the held-out test set — would support the hypothesis that sequential modeling of in-session behavior provides ranking lift over interaction-history-based MF under these conditions. It would not establish generalizability to other domains, nor prove that the Transformer would win under all session-length regimes (the stratified analysis informs this secondary question).

must_not_claim.claim: The test set spans only the final 3 months of a 18-month data window, which may not be sufficient to capture seasonal user behavior fluctuations.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: While longer test periods can improve robustness, a 3-month test set is standard in temporal evaluation of fashion domains and aligns with typical seasonal cycles. The stratification across device and session length ensures representativeness, and the hypothesis only requires evaluation on 'newly arriving sessions from a later time period'—which is satisfied.

must_not_claim.claim: Using NDCG@10 as the primary metric disadvantages models that generate diverse recommendations since it emphasizes top-ranked items.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: NDCG@10 is operationally appropriate here because stakeholder success hinges on surfacing purchased items within the typically visible recommendation surface (10–20 items). Rewarding high rankings aligns with business impact; diversity is captured via Coverage@10 as a secondary fairness metric.

must_not_claim.claim: The baseline model (LightFM) should not include item metadata features since the Transformer's advantage may stem from richer side information, not sequence modeling.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Including metadata in LightFM is correct because it ensures feature parity—this isolates sequence modeling as the independent variable. Without side features, the baseline would be structurally disadvantaged in handling metadata and cold-start items, conflating architecture fairness with feature access.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer acknowledges that the design is methodologically sound and raises no fatal flaws, while possibly noting non-critical observations that do not invalidate the conclusion.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The reviewer recognizes that temporal splitting prevents leakage, feature parity is preserved, and evaluation metrics align with operational goals. They decline to criticize legitimate choices such as hybrid LightFM or NDCG@10 usage.

acceptable_resolutions.description: If minor concerns are raised (e.g., about statistical power or cold-start handling), the defender successfully rebuts them by referencing design choices in the context of the hypothesis and data constraints.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The defender justifies stratification, computational budget equivalence, and single evaluation on test as sufficient for the stated scope. Critic fails to demonstrate actual methodology flaws.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Session-Based Transformer vs. Matrix Factorization for Next-Item Purchase Ranking

### 1. Objective and Scope

The goal is to determine whether a session-based Transformer recommender, trained on ordered in-session interaction sequences, produces superior next-item purchase ranking compared with a hybrid matrix factorization model trained on historical user-item interactions. The claim is scoped to future sessions arriving after the training window — it is not a claim about all recommendation settings, but specifically about the sequential, session-aware inference context of a live fashion marketplace.

### 2. Data Collection and Labeling

We would use 18 months of production event logs containing timestamped impressions, clicks, wishlist adds, add-to-cart events, and completed purchases for all sessions on web and mobile. Product metadata (category, brand, price, discount status, inventory flag) is extracted from daily catalog snapshots. Labels are directly observed from purchase events — no annotation is required. The ranking task is defined at the session level: given the interaction prefix before a purchase event, rank the catalog such that the purchased item ranks highly. Only sessions containing at least one purchase serve as labeled evaluation examples.

### 3. Temporal Split Strategy

Data is split strictly chronologically: months 1–12 for training, months 13–15 for validation, and months 16–18 as the held-out test set. A session is assigned to whichever split contains its first event timestamp. This temporal split is non-negotiable for this domain: fashion assortments rotate seasonally, promotions recur on predictable cycles, and user intent shifts with trends. A random split would allow models to see future seasonal signals during training, producing optimistic and unrealistic estimates. Stratification by device type and session-length bucket is enforced across splits to ensure distribution comparability.

### 4. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

For the Transformer, each session is represented as an ordered sequence of (item embedding, event type, time delta, metadata embeddings) tuples, capped at 50 events. For LightFM, the same item metadata features are passed as item side features in the hybrid formulation — this is the critical design decision ensuring both models have identical informational access to catalog attributes. All preprocessing (vocabulary construction, price normalization, frequency filters) is fit on the training partition only and applied to validation and test without refitting, preventing any leakage of future distribution statistics.

### 5. Models and Baseline

The proposed model is a SASRec-style 2-layer, 4-head causal Transformer trained with sampled softmax loss. The baseline is LightFM with identical item metadata side features — chosen specifically because it handles cold-start items through metadata embeddings, giving it structural parity with the Transformer on the cold-start dimension. Both models receive 50 hyperparameter search trials evaluated on validation NDCG@10. Anonymous sessions are handled identically by both models via a shared popularity-plus-category-affinity fallback.

### 6. Evaluation and Metrics

The primary metric is NDCG@10, computed against the full item catalog per session and averaged across all test sessions. NDCG@10 aligns with the stakeholder's goal of surfacing purchased items within the visible recommendation surface and rewards higher placements proportionally. Secondary metrics include HR@10, MRR, catalog Coverage@10 (to monitor fresh-item visibility), and stratified NDCG@10 by session length, device type, and cold-start status. Significance is assessed via paired Wilcoxon signed-rank test (α=0.05, Bonferroni-corrected).

### 7. Confound Controls

Three confounds receive explicit design controls. First, feature parity: LightFM's hybrid mode ensures the MF baseline has access to the same metadata, isolating sequence modeling as the independent variable. Second, catalog availability: both models use the same daily inventory snapshot at inference time, and cold-start NDCG@10 is reported separately. Third, popularity bias: metrics are computed against the full catalog rather than a sampled negative set, preventing well-known popularity-sampling artifacts from inflating scores. Results are further stratified by position in the test window to detect differential seasonal drift between models.

A positive result — statistically significant improvement in NDCG@10 on the held-out test set — would support the hypothesis that sequential modeling of in-session behavior provides ranking lift over interaction-history-based MF under these conditions. It would not establish generalizability to other domains, nor prove that the Transformer would win under all session-length regimes (the stratified analysis informs this secondary question).
difficulty_justification: The case is moderately challenging because it requires understanding nuanced trade-offs in recommender evaluation—such as the necessity of temporal splitting due to fashion seasonality, and the justification for including metadata in the baseline to ensure fair comparison—while avoiding false positives from overgeneralized best practices.

hypothesis: A transformer-based ranker using query, product text, image embeddings, and click-session context will surface more relevant products than a gradient-boosted ranker using only handcrafted catalog and user-behavior features, as measured by offline nDCG on held-out search sessions from a large e-commerce marketplace.
domain: E-commerce marketplace search and product discovery for a multilingual retail platform
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Transformer-Based Ranker vs. Gradient-Boosted Baseline

### 1. Objective and Hypothesis
We aim to test whether a transformer-based ranker using query embeddings, product text and image embeddings, and session context can surface more relevant products than a handcrafted-feature gradient-boosted ranker, as measured by offline nDCG@10 on manually annotated search sessions. Success means the transformer achieves significantly higher nDCG without sacrificing latency or increasing popularity bias.

### 2. Data Collection and Labeling Strategy

**Data source:** We would use 18 months of timestamped production search sessions from the e-commerce platform, encompassing millions of query–product impression pairs, clicks, add-to-cart events, purchases, and dwell times. Product metadata includes titles, descriptions, attributes, images, category hierarchy, and pricing.

**Gold-standard labels:** Position bias and implicit-feedback noise in raw clicks are well-known threats to ranking evaluation. To obtain clean relevance labels, we conduct manual annotation of a stratified sample of 5,000–10,000 test queries (sampled from months 14–18). Each query–product pair is labeled by two independent judges on a 5-point scale: irrelevant (0), marginally relevant (1), relevant (2), highly relevant (3), perfect match (4). We require inter-rater agreement (Krippendorff's α) ≥ 0.60 before proceeding. This annotated set is representative across languages, query types, and product categories, ensuring evaluation is not biased by data distribution shifts.

**Training labels:** For training, we use implicit relevance signals—clicks, add-to-cart events, and purchase conversions—with position-bias correction (e.g., inverse propensity weighting or variance correction based on impression position). This allows us to leverage the full training corpus without requiring manual annotation of millions of pairs.

### 3. Data Splitting and Temporal Alignment

We employ a **temporal split** to prevent leakage of future trends and to reflect real deployment conditions:
- **Train:** Months 1–10 (~10M sessions) — used to fit all models and preprocessors.
- **Validation:** Months 11–13 (~1M sessions) — used for hyperparameter tuning and model selection.
- **Test:** Months 14–18 (~1.5M sessions, with manually annotated subset for nDCG computation).

Within each split, we stratify by language, query category, and season to ensure balanced representation. Temporal splitting is critical because (1) it avoids encoding future information into training; (2) it reflects the real deployment scenario—models must generalize forward in time; (3) it prevents selection bias from random shuffling of time-dependent data.

### 4. Preprocessing and Feature Engineering

**Fit-on-train-only principle:** All preprocessing is fit exclusively on the training set and applied to validation and test without refitting.

**Transformer features:**
- Query embeddings: fine-tuned or frozen multilingual BERT (768-dim).
- Product text embeddings: encoder on product title + description (768-dim).
- Product image embeddings: frozen pre-trained vision model (ResNet-50 or CLIP, 2048-dim or 512-dim).
- Query–product interactions: element-wise product, absolute difference, cosine similarity.
- Session context: user click history over past N impressions, average dwell time, add-to-cart rate, language, product category.
- Product and temporal features: price, category depth, inventory, seller rating, impression rank, query-to-product time offset, day-of-week, seasonal indicator.

**Baseline features (handcrafted only):**
- Product features: price, category, inventory, seller rating, age.
- User-behavior aggregates: historical CTR by category, dwell-time distribution, add-to-cart frequency.
- Text overlap: Jaccard similarity, BM25 score, category match indicator.
- No embeddings, no images, no fine-tuned representations.

This ensures the baseline operates on a strict subset of signals, isolating the effect of embeddings and multimodality.

### 5. Model Training and Baseline Setup

**Transformer ranker:** A cross-encoder or dual-encoder architecture trained end-to-end on training sessions. The model jointly encodes query, product, and session context, outputs relevance scores for products in the impression list, and optimizes a listwise loss (e.g., LambdaMART or contrastive margin loss) to rank clicked items higher. Hyperparameters (learning rate, dropout, embedding dim, fine-tuning layers) are tuned via grid or random search on the validation set.

**Baseline (LightGBM or XGBoost):** Gradient-boosted tree ranker trained on the same training set with the same train/val/test splits. The baseline receives equivalent tuning effort: hyperparameter search over tree depth, L2 regularization, learning rate, sample weights, optimized on the same validation set. Both models are evaluated on identical test data using identical metrics.

### 6. Model Selection and Validation

1. Train the transformer and baseline on months 1–10.
2. Evaluate candidate hyperparameter configurations on the validation set (months 11–13) using nDCG@10 as the selection criterion.
3. Select the configuration with the highest validation nDCG for each model.
4. Refit the winning models on train + validation combined (months 1–13) to maximize training data.
5. Evaluate once on the held-out test set (months 14–18), computing nDCG@10 on the manually annotated subset. Report 95% confidence intervals via bootstrap resampling.
6. **Policy:** The test set is never accessed during model selection or tuning.

### 7. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric: nDCG@10** (on manually annotated test set).
- **Justification:** nDCG@10 directly measures ranking quality—the presence of highly relevant products in the top-10 positions. This aligns with the business objective (users see relevant products early, driving clicks and conversion) and the platform's display format (10 products per page). Manual annotations remove position bias and implicit-feedback noise, ensuring a clean comparison.

**Secondary metrics:**
- **MRR@10:** Mean reciprocal rank; captures how many results users must scan to find their first relevant item.
- **nDCG on position-bias-corrected implicit feedback:** Validates that improvement holds on unbiased click data, not just manual annotations.
- **Precision@k (k=1, 3, 5, 10):** Fraction of top-k results labeled relevant (≥3/5); operationally interpretable.
- **Latency (p50, p99):** Confirm inference time remains within SLA (e.g., <100ms p99).
- **Popularity bias drift:** Compare mean product popularity rank in top-10 predictions; ensure the transformer does not over-promote bestsellers.

### 8. Controls for Confounds

**Position bias:** Both models evaluated on position-independent manual labels. Position-bias-corrected implicit feedback is a secondary metric.

**Popularity bias:** We report the mean popularity rank of products in top-10 predictions for both models; a significant difference would indicate confounding by popularity.

**Richer metadata:** An ablated transformer variant (embeddings + text, no images) is trained to isolate the contribution of multimodal information from embedding quality.

**Language and seasonality:** Stratified evaluation reports nDCG@10 separately by language and season; improvement must be consistent across subgroups.

**Data leakage:** Temporal split and fit-on-train-only preprocessing prevent future-information leakage.

**Annotation quality:** Inter-rater agreement (α ≥ 0.60) is enforced before evaluation begins.

### 9. Scope and Claims

This design establishes whether a transformer ranker with embeddings and multimodal signals outperforms a handcrafted-feature baseline on manual relevance labels, in an offline setting, over an 18-month horizon. It does **not** establish: (1) online A/B test performance or conversion lift; (2) robustness to adversarial queries or catalog drift; (3) generalization to other e-commerce platforms. These require separate deployment and online experiments.

must_not_claim.claim: The test set (months 14–18) includes the same queries used for manual annotation, which could create circularity if annotations influence model training.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Manual annotations are used only for evaluation on the held-out test set, not for training. The model is trained on behavioral signals from months 1–13 with no access to manual labels, and preprocessing is fit only on training data. There is no feedback loop from annotations into training.

must_not_claim.claim: Refitting the final model on train + validation (months 1–13) leaks information into the model because the validation set was used for hyperparameter tuning.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Refitting the winning configuration on train + validation after selection is a standard best practice that improves final model robustness. Since the test set (months 14–18) remains untouched and the same protocol applies to both models, this does not invalidate the comparison or create leakage. The hyperparameter selection process itself only used the validation set once for each model.

must_not_claim.claim: Using nDCG@10 as the primary metric ignores long-tail relevance beyond the first page of results, which matters for some user intents.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: nDCG@10 is aligned with the platform's UI (10 products per page) and business goal (top-of-page relevance). While long-tail performance is important, it is captured in secondary analyses and metrics. The metric choice is appropriate for the stated objective.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer evaluates the design and concludes there are no fundamental flaws. The critic fails to identify any real corruptions because none exist. The defender successfully rebuts any spurious concerns by citing domain justifications and methodological best practices.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The critic raises concerns, but the defender shows that the choice is methodologically sound (e.g., temporal split prevents leakage, refit on train+val is standard, manual labels are isolated from training).

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer accepts that the baseline is fairly constructed despite using fewer features, recognizing that isolating the effect of embeddings and multimodal signals requires a restricted baseline.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The reviewer acknowledges that the baseline fairly represents a traditional, non-deep-learning approach using only handcrafted signals, making it a suitable comparison point for the hypothesis.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Transformer-Based Ranker vs. Gradient-Boosted Baseline

### 1. Objective and Hypothesis
We aim to test whether a transformer-based ranker using query embeddings, product text and image embeddings, and session context can surface more relevant products than a handcrafted-feature gradient-boosted ranker, as measured by offline nDCG@10 on manually annotated search sessions. Success means the transformer achieves significantly higher nDCG without sacrificing latency or increasing popularity bias.

### 2. Data Collection and Labeling Strategy

**Data source:** We would use 18 months of timestamped production search sessions from the e-commerce platform, encompassing millions of query–product impression pairs, clicks, add-to-cart events, purchases, and dwell times. Product metadata includes titles, descriptions, attributes, images, category hierarchy, and pricing.

**Gold-standard labels:** Position bias and implicit-feedback noise in raw clicks are well-known threats to ranking evaluation. To obtain clean relevance labels, we conduct manual annotation of a stratified sample of 5,000–10,000 test queries (sampled from months 14–18). Each query–product pair is labeled by two independent judges on a 5-point scale: irrelevant (0), marginally relevant (1), relevant (2), highly relevant (3), perfect match (4). We require inter-rater agreement (Krippendorff's α) ≥ 0.60 before proceeding. This annotated set is representative across languages, query types, and product categories, ensuring evaluation is not biased by data distribution shifts.

**Training labels:** For training, we use implicit relevance signals—clicks, add-to-cart events, and purchase conversions—with position-bias correction (e.g., inverse propensity weighting or variance correction based on impression position). This allows us to leverage the full training corpus without requiring manual annotation of millions of pairs.

### 3. Data Splitting and Temporal Alignment

We employ a **temporal split** to prevent leakage of future trends and to reflect real deployment conditions:
- **Train:** Months 1–10 (~10M sessions) — used to fit all models and preprocessors.
- **Validation:** Months 11–13 (~1M sessions) — used for hyperparameter tuning and model selection.
- **Test:** Months 14–18 (~1.5M sessions, with manually annotated subset for nDCG computation).

Within each split, we stratify by language, query category, and season to ensure balanced representation. Temporal splitting is critical because (1) it avoids encoding future information into training; (2) it reflects the real deployment scenario—models must generalize forward in time; (3) it prevents selection bias from random shuffling of time-dependent data.

### 4. Preprocessing and Feature Engineering

**Fit-on-train-only principle:** All preprocessing is fit exclusively on the training set and applied to validation and test without refitting.

**Transformer features:**
- Query embeddings: fine-tuned or frozen multilingual BERT (768-dim).
- Product text embeddings: encoder on product title + description (768-dim).
- Product image embeddings: frozen pre-trained vision model (ResNet-50 or CLIP, 2048-dim or 512-dim).
- Query–product interactions: element-wise product, absolute difference, cosine similarity.
- Session context: user click history over past N impressions, average dwell time, add-to-cart rate, language, product category.
- Product and temporal features: price, category depth, inventory, seller rating, impression rank, query-to-product time offset, day-of-week, seasonal indicator.

**Baseline features (handcrafted only):**
- Product features: price, category, inventory, seller rating, age.
- User-behavior aggregates: historical CTR by category, dwell-time distribution, add-to-cart frequency.
- Text overlap: Jaccard similarity, BM25 score, category match indicator.
- No embeddings, no images, no fine-tuned representations.

This ensures the baseline operates on a strict subset of signals, isolating the effect of embeddings and multimodality.

### 5. Model Training and Baseline Setup

**Transformer ranker:** A cross-encoder or dual-encoder architecture trained end-to-end on training sessions. The model jointly encodes query, product, and session context, outputs relevance scores for products in the impression list, and optimizes a listwise loss (e.g., LambdaMART or contrastive margin loss) to rank clicked items higher. Hyperparameters (learning rate, dropout, embedding dim, fine-tuning layers) are tuned via grid or random search on the validation set.

**Baseline (LightGBM or XGBoost):** Gradient-boosted tree ranker trained on the same training set with the same train/val/test splits. The baseline receives equivalent tuning effort: hyperparameter search over tree depth, L2 regularization, learning rate, sample weights, optimized on the same validation set. Both models are evaluated on identical test data using identical metrics.

### 6. Model Selection and Validation

1. Train the transformer and baseline on months 1–10.
2. Evaluate candidate hyperparameter configurations on the validation set (months 11–13) using nDCG@10 as the selection criterion.
3. Select the configuration with the highest validation nDCG for each model.
4. Refit the winning models on train + validation combined (months 1–13) to maximize training data.
5. Evaluate once on the held-out test set (months 14–18), computing nDCG@10 on the manually annotated subset. Report 95% confidence intervals via bootstrap resampling.
6. **Policy:** The test set is never accessed during model selection or tuning.

### 7. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric: nDCG@10** (on manually annotated test set).
- **Justification:** nDCG@10 directly measures ranking quality—the presence of highly relevant products in the top-10 positions. This aligns with the business objective (users see relevant products early, driving clicks and conversion) and the platform's display format (10 products per page). Manual annotations remove position bias and implicit-feedback noise, ensuring a clean comparison.

**Secondary metrics:**
- **MRR@10:** Mean reciprocal rank; captures how many results users must scan to find their first relevant item.
- **nDCG on position-bias-corrected implicit feedback:** Validates that improvement holds on unbiased click data, not just manual annotations.
- **Precision@k (k=1, 3, 5, 10):** Fraction of top-k results labeled relevant (≥3/5); operationally interpretable.
- **Latency (p50, p99):** Confirm inference time remains within SLA (e.g., <100ms p99).
- **Popularity bias drift:** Compare mean product popularity rank in top-10 predictions; ensure the transformer does not over-promote bestsellers.

### 8. Controls for Confounds

**Position bias:** Both models evaluated on position-independent manual labels. Position-bias-corrected implicit feedback is a secondary metric.

**Popularity bias:** We report the mean popularity rank of products in top-10 predictions for both models; a significant difference would indicate confounding by popularity.

**Richer metadata:** An ablated transformer variant (embeddings + text, no images) is trained to isolate the contribution of multimodal information from embedding quality.

**Language and seasonality:** Stratified evaluation reports nDCG@10 separately by language and season; improvement must be consistent across subgroups.

**Data leakage:** Temporal split and fit-on-train-only preprocessing prevent future-information leakage.

**Annotation quality:** Inter-rater agreement (α ≥ 0.60) is enforced before evaluation begins.

### 9. Scope and Claims

This design establishes whether a transformer ranker with embeddings and multimodal signals outperforms a handcrafted-feature baseline on manual relevance labels, in an offline setting, over an 18-month horizon. It does **not** establish: (1) online A/B test performance or conversion lift; (2) robustness to adversarial queries or catalog drift; (3) generalization to other e-commerce platforms. These require separate deployment and online experiments.
difficulty_justification: The case is challenging because the design is methodologically sound but includes several advanced ML practices (temporal split, refit on train+val, manual annotations, multimodal signals) that a less experienced reviewer might misinterpret as flaws. A correct evaluation requires deep understanding of offline ranking evaluation best practices in industrial settings.

hypothesis: A temporal transformer trained on clickstream sequences and session context will predict 7-day learner dropout from an online education platform more accurately than a baseline gradient-boosted model using only aggregated behavioral features, as measured on future cohorts.
domain: Online education platform learning analytics (student engagement and retention prediction from LMS activity logs)
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Temporal Transformer vs. Boosted Trees for Early Dropout Prediction

### 1. Data Collection and Labeling

We source 24 months of production event logs from the learning platform, capturing all clickstream events across all active courses. The raw data includes event type (page view, video play, quiz attempt, assignment submission, forum post), precise timestamp, session ID, learner ID, course ID, device type, and geolocation. This 24-month window is chosen to provide sufficient sample size across diverse learner cohorts and courses, and to ensure temporal splits do not create severe distribution mismatches between training and deployment.

The outcome is defined prospectively: for each learner-week reference point (end of week N, where the learner has been enrolled for at least 2 weeks), we label the learner as a dropout if they record zero platform events during the entire 7-day period of weeks N+1 through N+1. This definition captures the stakeholder's operational concern: identifying learners at risk of sustained disengagement. To avoid censoring bias, we exclude reference points in the final 2 weeks of the data collection period, ensuring all labels have complete 7-day observation windows.

### 2. Train-Validation-Test Split with Temporal and Course Stratification

The data is split temporally to prevent future-information leakage:
- Training set: months 1–12
- Validation set: months 13–18
- Test set: months 19–24

Within each temporal window, we stratify by course ID such that the distribution of course types (by enrollment size and pedagogical format—synchronous, asynchronous, blended) is preserved across all three sets. This respects the natural cohort structure of the platform: learner cohorts and dropout rates vary by course and term. Temporal stratification ensures the model is evaluated on future cohorts it has not seen and prevents the transformer or baseline from exploiting time-specific patterns that would not generalize.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Transformer model input (Feature Set A): Raw event sequences are extracted for each learner within the 7-day reference window. Each event is tokenized as a tuple: (event_type, time_since_previous_event_in_minutes, session_id, device_type, course_context). Sequences are padded to a fixed length of 512 tokens; sequences longer than 512 are truncated to the most recent 512 events. Event-type vocabulary is built from the training set; any event type appearing only in validation or test is mapped to a special UNK token.

Baseline model input (Feature Set B): Hand-crafted aggregates are computed over the same 7-day reference window: event count by type (5 features), session count (1), unique days active (1), distribution of event times across hours of day (24 bins), device diversity (count of unique device types), and intra-sequence statistics (mean time between events, coefficient of variation). These 35 features summarize learner activity during the reference week.

All preprocessing (scaling, encoding, vocabulary construction, imputation) is fit on the training set only. Scaling parameters (mean, standard deviation) are computed from training-set features and applied identically to validation and test. No information from validation or test data is used to fit any transformer or encoder.

### 4. Models and Baseline

Transformer model: A standard transformer encoder (6–12 layers, 8–16 attention heads, 512–768 hidden dimensions, absolute positional encodings) processes the event sequence (Feature Set A). Global average pooling aggregates the sequence of hidden states into a single vector. A 2-layer MLP head (with dropout regularization) maps this representation to a binary logit. The model is trained with binary cross-entropy loss on the training set.

Baseline model: A gradient-boosted decision tree ensemble (XGBoost or LightGBM) is trained on the hand-crafted aggregates (Feature Set B). Both models receive equivalent tuning effort: hyperparameter search is performed on the validation set using temporal cross-validation (see Validation Approach below), and the best hyperparameters from this search are then trained on the full training set and evaluated on the test set.

This design isolates the contribution of transformer modeling on sequences versus boosted trees on aggregates. If the transformer outperforms the baseline, it is because sequence structure and attention mechanisms capture dropout risk better than aggregate summaries—not because of disparate feature engineering or tuning effort.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

Primary metric: Precision at 90% recall, computed on the test set. The platform can operationally intervene with approximately 90% of at-risk students given current support staff capacity. Precision at this recall level quantifies the false-alarm rate: among the 90% of flagged learners, what fraction truly drop out? Higher precision means fewer wasted interventions per true positive, directly reducing staff burden and intervention cost. This operationally-grounded metric is superior to aggregate AUC, which can obscure precision-recall tradeoffs relevant to the deployment context.

Secondary metrics:
- Recall at 95% precision: the inverse view—if we enforce high precision (low false-alarm rate), how many at-risk learners do we catch?
- Area under the precision-recall curve (AUC-PR): a distribution-sensitive summary of performance across all thresholds, useful for detecting if one model systematically dominates the other.
- Expected calibration error (ECE) or Brier score: ensures predicted probabilities are well-calibrated, since interventions may be prioritized by model confidence.
- Per-course precision-recall curves: breakdown of performance by course type to detect whether the transformer's gains are universal or concentrated in specific courses (which could indicate overfitting to course structure rather than learner risk).

### 6. Model Selection and Validation

Hyperparameters for both models are tuned on the validation set (months 13–18) using 5-fold temporal stratified cross-validation. Folds are split chronologically within the validation window:
- Fold 1: train on months 13–14, validate on months 14–15
- Fold 2: train on months 13–15, validate on months 15–16
- Fold 3: train on months 13–16, validate on months 16–17
- Fold 4: train on months 13–17, validate on months 17–18
- Fold 5: train on months 13–18, validate on months 18–19 (using a small holdout)

Alternatively, a simpler approach: temporal split within validation (months 13–16 for tuning train, months 17–18 for tuning validation). The metric used for model selection is precision at 90% recall, averaged across folds. The model configuration achieving the highest mean validation precision at 90% recall is then trained on the full training set (months 1–12) and evaluated once on the test set (months 19–24). The test set is never touched during hyperparameter search.

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

Controlling for course-level heterogeneity: The temporal stratified split ensures both models are evaluated on the same distribution of course types. Per-course breakdowns of precision-recall performance will reveal whether the transformer's gains are uniform or concentrated in a few courses. If gains are skewed toward a subset of courses, this suggests the transformer is exploiting course-specific sequencing patterns rather than learning generalizable learner-level risk signals.

Controlling for label timing leakage: The prospective label definition (reference week N predicts week N+1) and exclusion of the final 2 weeks prevent training data from containing artificially easy labels near the collection boundary. Cross-validation folds are temporally ordered to prevent fold boundaries from inducing spurious leakage.

Equivalent feature and tuning effort: Both models receive the same split strategy, the same tuning methodology, and equivalent hyperparameter search budgets. The only difference is feature representation (sequences vs. aggregates) and model class (transformer vs. boosted trees). This isolates the hypothesis: does sequence modeling outperform aggregates?

Class imbalance handling: If dropout rates are severely imbalanced (<5% or >50%), stratified undersampling or class-weight rebalancing is applied to both models during training, applied identically. This is documented in the final report to ensure reproducibility.

Scope of conclusions: This design establishes whether a temporal transformer outperforms a gradient-boosted baseline for dropout prediction on this platform, across its current course portfolio, within the 24-month observation window. Results do not generalize to other platforms, new course types, or future terms with substantially different learner demographics. Temporal distribution shift (e.g., a new course format with different activity patterns) could degrade or reverse the relative performance. The design does not identify why the transformer performs better (e.g., which attention patterns drive predictions); ablation studies would be needed for mechanistic insight.

must_not_claim.claim: The training set (12 months) is much larger than the validation and test sets individually (6 and 6 months), which could lead to validation instability or test overfitting.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: In longitudinal educational data, split durations are determined by cohort availability and temporal structure, not by balancing split sizes. A 12-month training set is reasonable to capture long-term seasonality and rare course types, while the validation and test sets are already sufficiently large (6 months each) to cover multiple terms and provide stable performance estimates. The sound design uses temporal and course stratification to ensure distributional representativeness, not size symmetry.

must_not_claim.claim: The primary evaluation metric, precision at 90% recall, is non-standard and may obscure model performance compared to AUC.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Precision at 90% recall is appropriate here because it aligns with the stakeholder's operational constraint: the platform can only intervene on ~90% of at-risk learners. Maximizing precision under this recall constraint directly reduces false alarms and staff overhead. This is more policy-relevant than AUC, which aggregates over thresholds that may not represent deployable trade-offs.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer correctly identifies that the experimental design is valid and methodologically sound. No fundamental flaws are raised that cannot be rebutted using domain and methodological expertise. The defense successfully justifies the temporal split, metric choice, feature engineering, and confound controls as aligned with the hypothesis and deployment context.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The critique raises concerns that are either non-issues (e.g., test set size) or already controlled for in the design (e.g., course heterogeneity, leakage), and the defender provides accurate reasoning grounded in longitudinal learning analytics best practices.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The debate concludes in a draw because the critique raises a plausible-sounding concern, but the defender provides evidence from the design narrative (e.g., course stratification, prospective labeling) that resolves the concern and shows the risk is mitigated.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The critique questions a component (e.g., temporal cross-validation in validation window) that appears risky but is in fact correctly implemented per best practices in time-series ML evaluation. The defender demonstrates understanding by citing split integrity and temporal fold ordering.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Temporal Transformer vs. Boosted Trees for Early Dropout Prediction

### 1. Data Collection and Labeling

We source 24 months of production event logs from the learning platform, capturing all clickstream events across all active courses. The raw data includes event type (page view, video play, quiz attempt, assignment submission, forum post), precise timestamp, session ID, learner ID, course ID, device type, and geolocation. This 24-month window is chosen to provide sufficient sample size across diverse learner cohorts and courses, and to ensure temporal splits do not create severe distribution mismatches between training and deployment.

The outcome is defined prospectively: for each learner-week reference point (end of week N, where the learner has been enrolled for at least 2 weeks), we label the learner as a *dropout* if they record zero platform events during the entire 7-day period of weeks N+1 through N+1. This definition captures the stakeholder's operational concern: identifying learners at risk of sustained disengagement. To avoid censoring bias, we exclude reference points in the final 2 weeks of the data collection period, ensuring all labels have complete 7-day observation windows.

### 2. Train-Validation-Test Split with Temporal and Course Stratification

The data is split temporally to prevent future-information leakage:
- **Training set:** months 1–12
- **Validation set:** months 13–18
- **Test set:** months 19–24

Within each temporal window, we stratify by course ID such that the distribution of course types (by enrollment size and pedagogical format—synchronous, asynchronous, blended) is preserved across all three sets. This respects the natural cohort structure of the platform: learner cohorts and dropout rates vary by course and term. Temporal stratification ensures the model is evaluated on future cohorts it has not seen and prevents the transformer or baseline from exploiting time-specific patterns that would not generalize.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

**Transformer model input (Feature Set A):** Raw event sequences are extracted for each learner within the 7-day reference window. Each event is tokenized as a tuple: (event_type, time_since_previous_event_in_minutes, session_id, device_type, course_context). Sequences are padded to a fixed length of 512 tokens; sequences longer than 512 are truncated to the most recent 512 events. Event-type vocabulary is built from the training set; any event type appearing only in validation or test is mapped to a special UNK token.

**Baseline model input (Feature Set B):** Hand-crafted aggregates are computed over the same 7-day reference window: event count by type (5 features), session count (1), unique days active (1), distribution of event times across hours of day (24 bins), device diversity (count of unique device types), and intra-sequence statistics (mean time between events, coefficient of variation). These 35 features summarize learner activity during the reference week.

All preprocessing (scaling, encoding, vocabulary construction, imputation) is fit on the training set only. Scaling parameters (mean, standard deviation) are computed from training-set features and applied identically to validation and test. No information from validation or test data is used to fit any transformer or encoder.

### 4. Models and Baseline

**Transformer model:** A standard transformer encoder (6–12 layers, 8–16 attention heads, 512–768 hidden dimensions, absolute positional encodings) processes the event sequence (Feature Set A). Global average pooling aggregates the sequence of hidden states into a single vector. A 2-layer MLP head (with dropout regularization) maps this representation to a binary logit. The model is trained with binary cross-entropy loss on the training set.

**Baseline model:** A gradient-boosted decision tree ensemble (XGBoost or LightGBM) is trained on the hand-crafted aggregates (Feature Set B). Both models receive equivalent tuning effort: hyperparameter search is performed on the validation set using temporal cross-validation (see Validation Approach below), and the best hyperparameters from this search are then trained on the full training set and evaluated on the test set.

This design isolates the contribution of transformer modeling on sequences versus boosted trees on aggregates. If the transformer outperforms the baseline, it is because sequence structure and attention mechanisms capture dropout risk better than aggregate summaries—not because of disparate feature engineering or tuning effort.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** Precision at 90% recall, computed on the test set. The platform can operationally intervene with approximately 90% of at-risk students given current support staff capacity. Precision at this recall level quantifies the false-alarm rate: among the 90% of flagged learners, what fraction truly drop out? Higher precision means fewer wasted interventions per true positive, directly reducing staff burden and intervention cost. This operationally-grounded metric is superior to aggregate AUC, which can obscure precision-recall tradeoffs relevant to the deployment context.

**Secondary metrics:**
- Recall at 95% precision: the inverse view—if we enforce high precision (low false-alarm rate), how many at-risk learners do we catch?
- Area under the precision-recall curve (AUC-PR): a distribution-sensitive summary of performance across all thresholds, useful for detecting if one model systematically dominates the other.
- Expected calibration error (ECE) or Brier score: ensures predicted probabilities are well-calibrated, since interventions may be prioritized by model confidence.
- Per-course precision-recall curves: breakdown of performance by course type to detect whether the transformer's gains are universal or concentrated in specific courses (which could indicate overfitting to course structure rather than learner risk).

### 6. Model Selection and Validation

Hyperparameters for both models are tuned on the validation set (months 13–18) using 5-fold temporal stratified cross-validation. Folds are split chronologically within the validation window:
- Fold 1: train on months 13–14, validate on months 14–15
- Fold 2: train on months 13–15, validate on months 15–16
- Fold 3: train on months 13–16, validate on months 16–17
- Fold 4: train on months 13–17, validate on months 17–18
- Fold 5: train on months 13–18, validate on months 18–19 (using a small holdout)

Alternatively, a simpler approach: temporal split within validation (months 13–16 for tuning train, months 17–18 for tuning validation). The metric used for model selection is precision at 90% recall, averaged across folds. The model configuration achieving the highest mean validation precision at 90% recall is then trained on the full training set (months 1–12) and evaluated once on the test set (months 19–24). The test set is never touched during hyperparameter search.

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

**Controlling for course-level heterogeneity:** The temporal stratified split ensures both models are evaluated on the same distribution of course types. Per-course breakdowns of precision-recall performance will reveal whether the transformer's gains are uniform or concentrated in a few courses. If gains are skewed toward a subset of courses, this suggests the transformer is exploiting course-specific sequencing patterns rather than learning generalizable learner-level risk signals.

**Controlling for label timing leakage:** The prospective label definition (reference week N predicts week N+1) and exclusion of the final 2 weeks prevent training data from containing artificially easy labels near the collection boundary. Cross-validation folds are temporally ordered to prevent fold boundaries from inducing spurious leakage.

**Equivalent feature and tuning effort:** Both models receive the same split strategy, the same tuning methodology, and equivalent hyperparameter search budgets. The only difference is feature representation (sequences vs. aggregates) and model class (transformer vs. boosted trees). This isolates the hypothesis: does sequence modeling outperform aggregates?

**Class imbalance handling:** If dropout rates are severely imbalanced (<5% or >50%), stratified undersampling or class-weight rebalancing is applied to both models during training, applied identically. This is documented in the final report to ensure reproducibility.

**Scope of conclusions:** This design establishes whether a temporal transformer outperforms a gradient-boosted baseline for dropout prediction on *this platform, across its current course portfolio, within the 24-month observation window*. Results do not generalize to other platforms, new course types, or future terms with substantially different learner demographics. Temporal distribution shift (e.g., a new course format with different activity patterns) could degrade or reverse the relative performance. The design does not identify *why* the transformer performs better (e.g., which attention patterns drive predictions); ablation studies would be needed for mechanistic insight.
difficulty_justification: The case is moderately challenging because the design is correct but includes multiple complex components—temporal splitting, sequence modeling, and operational metrics—that could appear suspicious to a superficial reviewer. A good evaluator must distinguish best practices from violations, requiring deep understanding of longitudinal evaluation and stakeholder constraints.

hypothesis: A transformer-based code-risk model that combines source code, dependency metadata, and historical vulnerability fix patterns will predict high-risk open-source packages better than a model using source code alone, as measured by future confirmed CVE assignment in held-out time periods.
domain: Cybersecurity — open-source software supply-chain vulnerability risk scoring for public package registries such as npm or PyPI
task_prompt:
To test whether a transformer-based model incorporating source code, dependency metadata, and historical vulnerability-fix patterns outperforms a model using source code alone, we propose the following methodologically sound experiment:

**1. Data Collection and Labeling**

We will use a historical dataset spanning 4–5 years of public package registries (npm and/or PyPI). We target 50,000–100,000 packages with complete metadata: source code snapshots, dependency graphs, commit histories, and linked vulnerability records from NVD and GitHub Security Advisories. Labels are derived from CVE assignments: a package is labeled positive if it received a confirmed CVE within 12 months of the snapshot date; it is labeled negative if no CVE was discovered in that window and the package was not censored (i.e., had sufficient observation time or remained actively maintained). This definition reflects the realistic deployment scenario in which a model must predict future vulnerability disclosures.

**2. Temporal Split to Prevent Leakage**

Critically, we use a chronological split rather than random sampling. All package snapshots are dated; training data comprises snapshots from months 1–24, validation snapshots from months 25–30 (with forward labels from months 25–42), and test snapshots from months 31–36 (with forward labels from months 31–48). This temporal structure ensures the model never sees future CVE information during training or validation, reflecting the real-world scenario in which the model scores packages and must predict risks that will be discovered later. The 6-month gap between validation and test windows provides a buffer to further reduce potential bleed-through of disclosure patterns.

**3. Feature Engineering**

We construct three integrated feature groups: (a) source code features including AST statistics, token distributions, complexity metrics, and transformer-based embeddings (CodeBERT, fit on training code only); (b) dependency metadata (count, tree depth, version constraint patterns, transitive dependencies, and graph measures); and (c) historical patterns (commit frequency, recency, contributor count, commit message signals, and release velocity). All preprocessing (standardization, tokenization, embedding) is fit exclusively on the training set and applied without refitting to validation and test. This prevents information leakage.

**4. Model and Baselines**

The primary model is a transformer architecture that cross-attends source code embeddings, dependency metadata embeddings, and temporal feature vectors before a final classification head. We compare this against two fair baselines: (i) logistic regression on the full feature set (source code + metadata + history), receiving equal hyperparameter tuning effort; and (ii) logistic regression on source code features only. Both baselines use identical preprocessing, splits, and evaluation, ensuring a level comparison.

**5. Evaluation Metrics**

The primary metric is PR-AUC, selected because the task exhibits severe class imbalance (CVEs are rare: 2–5% base rate) and the operational objective is high precision under a constrained alert budget. PR-AUC is directly sensitive to the precision-recall tradeoff critical for security operations. Secondary metrics include precision at fixed recall levels (e.g., 80% recall), ROC-AUC, calibration error, and false positive rate, to provide operational context and sanity checks.

**6. Model Selection and Test Protocol**

Hyperparameter tuning (learning rate, regularization, class weighting) is performed by grid search on the validation set PR-AUC only. The test set is locked and evaluated exactly once after all tuning is complete. No threshold adjustment or re-weighting is applied post-hoc based on test results.

**7. Confound Controls**

To address the risk that the model learns package popularity or maintainer activity rather than true vulnerability risk, we perform three checks on the test set: (i) stratified analysis reporting PR-AUC separately by commit frequency and dependency count; (ii) ablation comparing the full transformer against a metadata-and-history-only variant; and (iii) correlation of model predictions with popularity proxies (downloads, dependents). These checks will reveal whether the lift is driven by vulnerability patterns or confounding factors.

**Claims and Scope**

This design will establish whether the transformer model with integrated features achieves higher PR-AUC than the baseline models on held-out temporal test data, under realistic label noise and censoring. It will not establish causation (correlation with a latent vulnerability-generation process), and results may be limited by label incompleteness (many vulnerabilities are never disclosed). The design is robust to temporal leakage and ensures fair comparison across models.

must_not_claim.claim: The test set (6 months) is too short compared to the training set (24 months), risking insufficient statistical power or poor generalization.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The test set duration matches the evaluation window (12-month risk horizon per snapshot), not the training duration. Each test snapshot uses a 12-month forward observation period (months 31–48), giving full label maturity. The chronological split ensures realistic deployment simulation, and 6 months of snapshots provide tens of thousands of data points given the scale of public registries.

must_not_claim.claim: Using PR-AUC as the primary metric disadvantages models that are well-calibrated or have high recall, and could mask poor generalization.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: PR-AUC is the appropriate metric for highly imbalanced risk prediction tasks in security contexts, where precision under limited alert capacity is prioritized. The hypothesis specifically aims to improve actionable prediction under imbalance, making PR-AUC more informative than ROC-AUC. Secondary metrics (calibration, FPR, precision at recall) provide complementary evaluation.

must_not_claim.claim: Logistic regression is not a fair baseline because it cannot model complex interactions in source code like a transformer.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The baselines are designed to be fair: one uses the same full feature set as the transformer (ensuring input parity), and the other isolates source-code-only performance as specified in the hypothesis. The comparison evaluates whether added complexity (transformer + integration) yields gains beyond simpler, well-tuned models, not whether it beats a weak baseline.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic fails to identify any flaws; the defender successfully argues that the design prevents temporal leakage, uses appropriate metrics for imbalance, and includes fair baselines and confound checks. The final verdict is that the experiment is valid for testing the hypothesis.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The temporal split respects causality; preprocessing is fitted only on training; PR-AUC aligns with operational goals; baselines are equitably constructed; confound checks are appropriate and pre-specified.

sound_design_reference:

The challenge lies in distinguishing between legitimately defensive decisions (e.g., PR-AUC for imbalance, temporal split) and actual flaws—of which there are none—thus testing the reviewer's ability to correctly validate rather than default to criticism.

hypothesis: For hospital pathology workflows, a document-level transformer fine-tuned on full-text pathology reports will achieve higher micro-F1 for multi-label ICD-O morphology and topography code assignment than a TF-IDF plus one-vs-rest linear baseline, especially on rare but clinically important cancer subtypes.
domain: Healthcare clinical NLP for cancer registry abstraction from hospital pathology reports
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Transformer vs. TF-IDF Baseline for Multi-Label ICD-O Code Assignment

### 1. Data and Labels

The experiment uses de-identified pathology reports from a multi-site health system tumor registry, covering 8 years (2015–2022) and targeting a minimum of 50,000 finalized reports across at least 3 hospital sites. Labels are registrar-assigned ICD-O-3 morphology and topography codes drawn from the finalized cancer registry record — not draft or preliminary assignments — ensuring that every labeled example reflects the ground truth a coding assistance system would need to replicate. Reports lacking finalized coding status are excluded. Codes appearing fewer than 10 times in the full dataset are flagged as ultra-rare; they are retained in training and evaluation but tracked in a separate stratum to support the rare-subtype sub-claim.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split temporally: 2015–2019 for training (~62%), 2020 for validation (~13%), and 2021–2022 for the held-out test set (~25%). A temporal split is non-negotiable here because coding practices evolve with synoptic template adoption and registry guideline updates; a random split would allow the model to observe future coding conventions during training and produce optimistically biased estimates. Every hospital site appears in all three partitions to prevent site identity from being a perfect predictor of split membership. Patient-level grouping is enforced: all reports from a single patient reside in exactly one partition, eliminating label leakage for patients with multiple specimens.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Both models receive the same cleaned report text. For the transformer, a structured truncation strategy is applied: the final diagnosis/impression section is placed first in the input window, followed by microscopic description, gross description, and clinical history, filling up to the model's token limit. This ensures that diagnostic evidence — which appears at the report's end in most pathology formats — is not silently dropped by naive head-truncation. For the TF-IDF baseline, unigrams and bigrams are extracted with sublinear TF scaling and a vocabulary cap of 100,000 features. All fitting — TF-IDF IDF weights, label binarizer, rare-code thresholds — occurs exclusively on the training partition and is applied frozen to validation and test.

### 4. Models and Baseline

Two transformer architectures are evaluated on the validation set: ClinicalBERT/BioBERT (512-token window, structured truncation) and Longformer (4096-token window). The better-performing architecture on validation micro-F1 becomes the transformer representative. Fine-tuning uses a sigmoid multi-label head with binary cross-entropy loss; per-label positive class weights (computed from training set frequency) address label imbalance. Hyperparameters swept on validation: learning rate ∈ {1e-5, 2e-5, 3e-5}, batch size ∈ {16, 32}, and classification head dropout.

The baseline is TF-IDF with one-vs-rest logistic regression, with regularization strength C swept over {0.01, 0.1, 1, 10} using the same validation micro-F1 criterion. This is the correct baseline because it reflects the practical state-of-the-art for automated registry coding assistance — fast, interpretable, and strong on high-frequency tokens — and isolates the contribution of learned contextualized representations. Both models use identical text input and label schema; neither receives site or date features.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is micro-F1 over all ICD-O morphology and topography labels jointly. Micro-F1 is appropriate because it weights labels by support, reflecting total registry throughput — every correct code assignment reduces manual effort proportionally. It is evaluated at a fixed 0.5 threshold and at the validation-optimized threshold. Secondary metrics include: macro-F1 (equal weight to rare and common codes), micro-F1 restricted to codes with fewer than 100 training examples (the rare-code stratum directly testing the hypothesis's key sub-claim), per-label F1 for 20 pre-specified clinically critical rare codes, macro-averaged AUPRC (threshold-independent), label-wise calibration via expected calibration error, and site-stratified micro-F1 to check geographic generalizability.

### 6. Validation and Test Policy

All model selection decisions are made on the 2020 validation set. The test set is evaluated exactly once after all tuning is complete. Results are reported with 95% bootstrap confidence intervals and McNemar's test for pairwise comparison between transformer and baseline predictions.

### 7. Confound Controls

Three confounds receive explicit treatment. First, the site-template confound: site identity is withheld from both models, and site-stratified evaluation checks whether gains are concentrated at a single site with distinctive boilerplate. Second, the time-period artifact confound: the temporal split structurally prevents future coding conventions from leaking into training; an ablation compares transformer performance on early vs. late test-year reports to probe temporal drift sensitivity. Third, a post-hoc interpretability audit samples rare-code predictions and reviews transformer attention weights and TF-IDF coefficients with a clinical co-investigator, assessing whether model attention aligns with diagnostically meaningful text versus administrative templates.

This experiment establishes whether a fine-tuned document-level transformer outperforms a well-tuned TF-IDF+OvR baseline for multi-label ICD-O coding in a temporally valid, multi-site setting. It does not establish clinical deployment readiness, does not test prospective performance, and does not rule out that hybrid or ensemble approaches might narrow the gap further.

must_not_claim.claim: The temporal split introduces a distribution shift that invalidates generalization to future years.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Temporal splits are essential in clinical NLP studies where coding practices evolve; the risk of optimistic bias from random splits outweighs the difficulty of future generalization, which is mitigated by longitudinal test set coverage (2021–2022) and explicit temporal drift analysis in confound controls.

must_not_claim.claim: The TF-IDF baseline is not strong enough — modern sparse baselines use advanced n-gram features or external knowledge, so this comparison overstates transformer gains.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The TF-IDF + OvR logistic regression represents the current practical state-of-the-art in real registry deployments: it is fast, interpretable, and widely implemented. A strong but realistically constrained baseline ensures policy-relevant conclusions.

must_not_claim.claim: The structured truncation for transformers introduces bias by prioritizing certain sections, altering the input distribution.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Structured truncation preserves diagnostically critical content that appears at the end of pathology reports. Without it, models trained on fixed-length inputs would lose key evidence — a domain-appropriate adjustment essential for validity in long-document classification.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer evaluates the design and concludes that no flaws are present. The defender successfully rebuts any attempted critiques by referencing domain-specific justifications (e.g., necessity of temporal split, appropriateness of baseline). The final verdict endorses the design as valid for testing the hypothesis.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The design conforms to the sound reference: preprocessing is fit only on training data, test set is unused during selection, confound controls are present, and metric aligns with stakeholder goals.

hypothesis: For a retail bank’s fraud detection pipeline, a sequence model trained on raw card-transaction event histories and merchant embeddings will detect next-day fraudulent transactions with higher precision at the same recall than a gradient-boosted model trained on aggregated account-level features.
domain: Retail banking fraud detection on card transaction streams
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Sequence Model vs. Gradient-Boosted Model for Card Fraud Detection

### 1. Data and Labeling

The experiment uses 18–24 months of production card transaction logs from the retail bank, including all authorized and declined transactions. Each record contains transaction timestamp, amount, merchant category (MCC), channel (online/in-person/ATM), geolocation, device/token identifier, and chargeback-confirmed fraud labels assigned within 90 days post-transaction. Transactions without a resolved label after 90 days are excluded to avoid label noise. This dataset reflects the deployment distribution: millions of transactions, highly imbalanced (typically 0.1–0.3% fraud rate), and subject to concept drift as fraud tactics evolve.

### 2. Temporal Split Strategy

Data is divided chronologically without shuffling: months 1–12 for training, months 13–15 for validation, months 16–18 for test. This temporal split is mandatory for fraud detection because predictions must use only historical information available at decision time. Shuffling or stratified random sampling would leak future fraud patterns into training, inflating performance estimates and violating causal ordering. The split mirrors production deployment: a model trained on months 1–12 is deployed to predict on month 13 onward. Stratification by fraud prevalence across windows confirms similar fraud rates; gross imbalances are flagged and documented.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

For the sequence model, transactions are represented as sequences per account, with each event encoded as (timestamp, log_amount, merchant_embedding, channel_onehot, geoloc_region). Merchant embeddings are learned from co-occurrence patterns in the training set using Word2Vec or via the embedding layer of the neural network. Sequences are truncated or zero-padded to a fixed length (e.g., 100 recent transactions per account) to enable batching. For the baseline gradient-boosted model, features are aggregated account-level statistics over 30-, 60-, and 90-day rolling windows: transaction count, total and average amount, frequency, unique merchant count, channel diversity, geographic diversity, device diversity, and interaction terms.

All preprocessing transformers (scaling, categorical encoding, embedding vocabularies) are fit on the training window only and applied identically to validation and test to prevent data leakage and ensure realistic deployment conditions.

### 4. Model Selection and Tuning

**Sequence model:** A bidirectional LSTM or Transformer with attention. Architecture: embedding layer (merchant, 64d), LSTM/Transformer (128 hidden, 2 layers, dropout=0.3), attention pooling, and binary classification head. Trained end-to-end on training data using cross-entropy loss with class-weight balancing. Hyperparameters (learning rate, dropout, sequence length, embedding dimension) are tuned via grid or random search on the validation window, maximizing precision at recall=0.80.

**Baseline:** Gradient-boosted model (XGBoost or LightGBM) on aggregated features. Hyperparameter tuning uses the same validation data and optimization metric as the sequence model, ensuring equivalent tuning effort and fair comparison. Class weights are applied to handle imbalance.

### 5. Evaluation Strategy

**Primary metric:** Precision at fixed recall (recall=0.80). Justification: The stakeholder's constraint is investigator capacity and false-decline cost. Precision at a meaningful recall level quantifies how many alerts are true positives—directly mapping to business objective. The recall threshold (0.80) is set on validation data via the precision-recall curve and applied uniformly to both models' test predictions.

**Secondary metrics:** Recall at fixed precision (e.g., precision=0.95), area under the precision-recall curve (AUPRC), precision and recall by fraud amount quartiles, false positive and false negative rates stratified by time (detect drift), and calibration error.

### 6. Validation and Test Protocol

Hyperparameter selection uses only the validation window (months 13–15). The test window (months 16–18) is locked and accessed only once. After selection, both models are retrained on training + validation combined, then evaluated on the held-out test set. Final results are reported on test data only; confidence intervals (95%) are computed via bootstrap resampling.

### 7. Confound Controls

To isolate sequential modeling value from simple time-windowed features or recency bias: (1) Train an ablated sequence model without learned merchant embeddings to test if one-hot merchants suffice. (2) Extract LSTM attention weights and SHAP values to verify that predictions align with known fraud vectors, not just transaction frequency. (3) Augment the baseline with recent-history features (e.g., transaction count in last 7 days); if the gap closes, the advantage comes from feature engineering, not sequential structure. (4) Stratify test performance by month and fraud cohort to detect overfitting to a particular wave or season. (5) Report performance separately for in-distribution vs. out-of-distribution account cohorts to quantify extrapolation risk.

### 8. Scope and Claims

This experiment establishes whether a sequence model with learned merchant embeddings achieves higher precision at a fixed recall than a well-tuned gradient-boosted model on aggregated features, within the bank's transaction data distribution and fraud label quality. It does not establish whether the sequence model generalizes to other fraud types, customer segments, or geographies unless explicitly held-out and tested. Any observed gain is attributed to the model class and feature representation; claims about why are supported only by ablations and attention analysis.

must_not_claim.claim: The temporal split (months 1–12, 13–15, 16–18) appears unbalanced in duration and could reduce training data size, potentially weakening model performance.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Temporal splits must preserve chronological order in fraud detection to avoid future leakage. The 12-month training window is justified by data volume and drift patterns; reducing it would harm validity. The design correctly prioritizes causal integrity over training data maximization.

must_not_claim.claim: Using precision at fixed recall (0.80) as the primary metric ignores the stakeholder's potential tolerance for higher false positives.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The primary metric is chosen based on stakeholder constraints — limited investigator bandwidth — which makes precision at fixed (operationally meaningful) recall the most decision-relevant metric. This is standard practice in operational fraud systems where alert volume must be controlled.

must_not_claim.claim: The sequence model uses merchant embeddings, which could introduce bias or overfit to rare merchant patterns not seen in production.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Merchant embeddings are learned solely from the training set and evaluated under temporal split conditions that mimic deployment. Their use is a core part of the hypothesis being tested — whether such representations improve performance — and does not constitute a flaw if properly contained.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic raises a concern, the defender provides rationale grounded in temporal causality, operational constraints, or fair comparison principles, and the final verdict affirms the design as valid and sound.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The defender correctly invokes the necessity of temporal splits to prevent data leakage, the business relevance of precision-at-recall for constrained investigator capacity, or the legitimacy of embedding-based representations when trained and evaluated under proper causality constraints.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic identifies a potential risk, the defender acknowledges it and references the relevant confound control (e.g., ablation or SHAP analysis), and the critique is downgraded as the concern is already addressed in the design.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The defender shows that the alleged risk — such as recency bias or merchant overfitting — is explicitly controlled via one or more of the listed confound controls (e.g., ablated embedding model, attention analysis, augmented baseline).
